abstract: Agent Skills are structured packages of procedural knowledge that augment LLM agents at inference time. Despite rapid adoption, there is no standard way to measure whether they actually help. We present SkillsBench, a benchmark of 86 tasks across 11 domains paired with curated Skills and deterministic verifiers. Each task is evaluated under three conditions: no Skills, curated Skills, and self-generated Skills. We test 7 agent-model configurations over 7,308 trajectories. Curated Skills raise average pass rate by 16.2 percentage points(pp), but effects vary widely by domain (+4.5pp for Software Engineering to +51.9pp for Healthcare) and 16 of 84 tasks show negative deltas. Self-generated Skills provide no benefit on average, showing that models cannot reliably author the procedural knowledge they benefit from consuming. Focused Skills with 2--3 modules outperform comprehensive documentation, and smaller models with Skills can match larger models without them.

keyInsights: Curated Agent Skills provide substantial but variable performance benefits to LLM agents, with an average improvement of +16.2 percentage points, depending on the agent-model combination and domain.
Self-generated Skills by LLMs yield negligible or negative performance changes compared to human-curated Skills, indicating models struggle to reliably author effective procedural knowledge.
Skills can effectively substitute for model scale, allowing smaller LLMs augmented with Skills to achieve performance comparable to larger models without Skills, suggesting cost-effectiveness.
originalProblem: A lack of systematic evaluation for the efficacy of "Agent Skills" in augmenting large language model (LLM) agents at inference time.
Existing agent benchmarks primarily assess raw model capabilities in isolation, rather than quantifying the value added by external procedural knowledge.
Practitioners and researchers lacked empirical data to guide the adoption, design, and development of effective Agent Skills.
results: Curated Skills improved average agent performance by +16.2 percentage points across 7 model-harness configurations, with the largest absolute improvement of +23.3pp.

Skills benefit varied across domains, with Healthcare (+51.9pp) and Manufacturing (+41.9pp) showing the largest gains, while Mathematics (+6.0pp) and Software Engineering (+4.5pp) saw smaller improvements.
Optimal Skills quantity was found to be 2-3 Skills per task (+18.6pp), and moderate-length Skills provided larger benefits than comprehensive, verbose ones (which resulted in a -2.9pp delta).
solution: Developed SKILLSBENCH, a containerized evaluation framework that assesses every task under both vanilla (no Skills) and Skills-augmented conditions to directly measure efficacy.

Defined a structured "Skill" artifact as procedural content for a class of problems, distinguishing it from system prompts or few-shot examples, and comprising instructions and optional executable resources.
Constructed a diverse dataset of 84 tasks across 11 domains with human-authored instructions, emphasizing Skill generality, deterministic verification, and strict quality filtering for submissions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduced SkillsBench, a benchmark to evaluate how effectively "Agent Skills" augment large language model (LLM) agents, measuring performance across diverse tasks and agent-model configurations. The benchmark found that curated Skills improved average agent performance by +16.2 percentage points and can enable smaller LLMs to match the performance of larger models operating without Skills.

title: SkillsBench: Benchmarking How Well Agent Skills Work Across Diverse Tasks

abstract:
We introduce REAL, a benchmark and framework for multi-turn agent evaluations
on deterministic simulations of real-world websites. REAL comprises
high-fidelity, deterministic replicas of 11 widely-used websites across domains
such as e-commerce, travel, communication, and professional networking. We also
release a benchmark consisting of 112 practical tasks that mirror everyday
complex user interactions requiring both accurate information retrieval and
state-changing actions. All interactions occur within this fully controlled
setting, eliminating safety risks and enabling robust, reproducible evaluation
of agent capability and reliability. Our novel evaluation framework combines
programmatic checks of website state for action-based tasks with rubric-guided
LLM-based judgments for information retrieval. The framework supports both
open-source and proprietary agent systems through a flexible evaluation harness
that accommodates black-box commands within browser environments, allowing
research labs to test agentic systems without modification. Our empirical
results show that frontier language models achieve at most a 41% success rate
on REAL, highlighting critical gaps in autonomous web navigation and task
completion capabilities. Our framework supports easy integration of new tasks,
reproducible evaluation, and scalable post-training data generation, marking a
significant step forward in evaluating and advancing agent capabilities.

keyInsights: REAL successfully bridges the gap between realism and reproducibility, enabling controlled experimentation on complex web environments.
The benchmark identifies critical deficiencies in current LLM-powered agents, particularly regarding failure recognition, state verification, and recovery from navigation dead ends.
The deterministic nature and robust reward system of REAL provide a suitable environment for generating training data and applying reinforcement learning to improve web agents.
originalProblem: Existing web agent benchmarks often use simplified interfaces or artificial tasks, failing to reflect real-world complexity and utility.
The inherent non-determinism of live websites makes reproducible evaluation nearly impossible and introduces safety concerns for agent interaction.
Prior environments lack the necessary fidelity, configurability, and clear reward signals required for effectively training and evaluating large language model-powered agents.
results: The best-performing model, Claude 3.7 Sonnet-Thinking, achieved a 41.07% success rate across all tasks, with Gemini 2.5 Pro Experimental at 38.39%.
Open-source models generally lagged, with Llama-4-Maverick achieving 12.50% and OpenAI’s Computer-Using Agent (CUA) scoring only 7.14%.
Analysis revealed common failure modes including agents not verifying actual website state post-action and getting stuck in non-standard navigation flows without recovery strategies.
solution: The REAL framework provides 11 publicly hosted, high-fidelity replicas of consumer websites, designed to be fully deterministic for reproducible experimentation.
It includes 112 realistic, multi-turn tasks across diverse domains, evaluated using a combination of programmatic state verification and LLM-based judging.

The system offers configurable environments via URL parameters to simulate specific scenarios and edge cases, alongside a flexible agent harness supporting various interaction models like Playwright and Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP).
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed REAL, a benchmark featuring 11 deterministic, high-fidelity simulations of real-world websites and 112 multi-turn tasks to evaluate autonomous web agents. The evaluation of frontier language models on REAL revealed that no agent achieved higher than a 41.07% success rate, indicating significant limitations in current capabilities.

title: REAL: Benchmarking Autonomous Agents on Deterministic Simulations of  Real Websites

abstract: We introduce MCP-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) on realistic, multi-step tasks that demand tool use, cross-tool coordination, precise parameter control, and planning/reasoning for solving tasks. Built on the Model Context Protocol (MCP), MCP-Bench connects LLMs to 28 representative live MCP servers spanning 250 tools across domains such as finance, traveling, scientific computing, and academic search. Unlike prior API-based benchmarks, each MCP server provides a set of complementary tools designed to work together, enabling the construction of authentic, multi-step tasks with rich input-output coupling. Tasks in MCP-Bench test agents' ability to retrieve relevant tools from fuzzy instructions without explicit tool names, plan multi-hop execution trajectories for complex objectives, ground responses in intermediate tool outputs, and orchestrate cross-domain workflows - capabilities not adequately evaluated by existing benchmarks that rely on explicit tool specifications, shallow few-step workflows, and isolated domain operations. We propose a multi-faceted evaluation framework covering tool-level schema understanding and usage, trajectory-level planning, and task completion. Experiments on 20 advanced LLMs reveal persistent challenges in MCP-Bench. Code and data: this https URL.

keyInsights: Higher-order reasoning, particularly long-horizon planning, dependency awareness, and efficient resource utilization, represents the most significant performance gap among LLM agents, rather than basic tool invocation.
The ability to handle 'fuzzy instructions' and infer appropriate tools from ambiguous natural language is a critical differentiator for top-performing models in real-world scenarios.

Multi-server and cross-domain orchestration tasks remain challenging for LLM agents, with weaker models showing noticeable degradation when required to coordinate distributed workflows and interdependent states.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for tool-using LLMs often provide isolated functionalities or simplistic, few-step tasks, lacking realistic input-output dependencies and natural complexity.

Prior evaluations fail to adequately test agents on critical real-world challenges such as inferring appropriate tools from ambiguous instructions, coordinating multiple objectives, or orchestrating tools across different domains.
Many current benchmarks do not rigorously assess information grounding, making it difficult to determine if agents' responses are based on actual tool outputs or hallucination.
results: Top-tier models (`gpt-5`, `o3`) achieved the highest overall scores (0.749 and 0.715 respectively) but still demonstrated limitations in planning and cross-domain tasks.
Basic capabilities such as schema compliance and valid tool naming converged for top models, exceeding 98% accuracy, indicating these are no longer primary bottlenecks.

The 'Planning Effectiveness' axis showed the sharpest disparities, with `gpt-5` scoring 0.76 in dependency awareness while smaller models rarely exceeded 0.30, highlighting persistent challenges in strategic reasoning.
solution: MCP-Bench leverages the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to connect LLM agents to 28 live, production-grade servers, exposing 250 structured tools across eleven diverse functional domains.

A three-stage LLM-based pipeline generates complex, multi-hop tasks by analyzing tool dependency chains, emphasizing cross-server coordination, and 'fuzzifying' task descriptions to omit explicit tool names.

A multi-tier evaluation framework combines objective rule-based checks for execution robustness with nuanced LLM-as-a-Judge scoring across task completion, tool usage, and planning effectiveness, incorporating bias mitigation via prompt shuffling.
paper_summary.summary: Accenture and UC Berkeley researchers developed MCP-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on complex, multi-step real-world tasks using production-grade Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers and fuzzy instructions. The evaluation of 20 LLMs revealed that while basic tool execution is robust, advanced models still face challenges in long-horizon planning, cross-domain orchestration, and information grounding.

title: MCP-Bench: Benchmarking Tool-Using LLM Agents with Complex Real-World Tasks via MCP Servers

abstract: The potential of Large Language Model (LLM) as agents has been widely acknowledged recently. Thus, there is an urgent need to quantitatively \textit{evaluate LLMs as agents} on challenging tasks in interactive environments. We present AgentBench, a multi-dimensional benchmark that consists of 8 distinct environments to assess LLM-as-Agent's reasoning and decision-making abilities. Our extensive test over \num API-based and open-sourced (OSS) LLMs shows that, while top commercial LLMs present a strong ability of acting as agents in complex environments, there is a significant disparity in performance between them and many OSS competitors that are no larger than 70B. We identify the typical reasons of failures in environments and LLMs, showing that poor long-term reasoning, decision-making, and instruction following abilities are the main obstacles for developing usable LLM agents. Improving instruction following and training on high quality multi-round alignment data could improve agent performance. And different from existing assumptions, training on code present ambivalent impacts on different agent tasks. Datasets, environments, and an integrated evaluation package for AgentBench are released at this https URL.

keyInsights: High-quality alignment data is crucial for developing capable LLM agents, with models like Vicuna-13B achieving competitive scores comparable to larger models due to superior alignment.

Code-tuning exhibits a nuanced effect on agent performance, benefiting static procedural tasks (e.g., Web Shopping) but showing ambivalent or detrimental impacts on tasks requiring general thinking or complex interaction beyond pure code generation (e.g., Digital Card Game).

Robust internal planning and error recovery are essential for effective agency, as top models maintain more coherent thought processes and exhibit self-correction, unlike others that fall into repetitive cycles when encountering errors.
originalProblem: A critical lack of systematic, standardized benchmarks existed for evaluating LLMs in diverse, interactive, and real-world-aligned agent environments.
Prior benchmarks were often confined to single tasks or specific domains, failing to provide a holistic view of an LLM's comprehensive agentic capabilities.
Existing evaluation methods suffered from limitations like modality mismatch for text-only LLMs and the absence of unified, publicly accessible tools for comparative analysis.
results: A significant performance disparity was observed, with top commercial LLMs (e.g., GPT-4 achieving an overall AGENTBENCH score of 4.01) substantially outperforming open-source models (average OSS score of 0.51).

"Task Limit Exceeded" was identified as the dominant failure mode, indicating models frequently fail to solve problems within interaction rounds or fall into repetitive generation loops (over 90% of TLE trajectories showed repetition).
GPT-4 led in 6 out of 8 environments, demonstrating strong practical usability in tasks such as House Holding with a 78% success rate, showcasing its advanced agentic capabilities.
solution: Developed AGENTBENCH, a multi-dimensional benchmark comprising 8 distinct, interactive, text-only environments categorized into code-grounded, game-grounded, and web-grounded scenarios.

Formalized the interactive evaluation of LLMs as agents within a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) framework, employing a 1-shot Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting strategy with greedy decoding for consistent results.
Evaluated 29 diverse LLMs using a unified, scalable Server-Client evaluation toolkit that encapsulates complex task environments in Docker images and supports resumable evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: AGENTBENCH introduces a multi-dimensional benchmark with 8 interactive environments to systematically evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) as agents. The benchmark reveals a significant performance gap between commercial and open-source LLMs, identifying predominant failure modes in long-term reasoning and instruction following.

title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents

abstract: Real-world language agents must handle complex, multi-step workflows across diverse Apps. For instance, an agent may manage emails by coordinating with calendars and file systems, or monitor a production database to detect anomalies and generate reports following an operating manual. However, existing language agent benchmarks often focus on narrow domains or simplified tasks that lack the diversity, realism, and long-horizon complexity required to evaluate agents' real-world performance. To address this gap, we introduce the Tool Decathlon (dubbed as Toolathlon), a benchmark for language agents offering diverse Apps and tools, realistic environment setup, and reliable execution-based evaluation. Toolathlon spans 32 software applications and 604 tools, ranging from everyday platforms such as Google Calendar and Notion to professional ones like WooCommerce, Kubernetes, and BigQuery. Most of the tools are based on a high-quality set of Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers that we may have revised or implemented ourselves. Unlike prior works, which primarily ensure functional realism but offer limited environment state diversity, we provide realistic initial environment states from real software, such as Canvas courses with dozens of students or real financial spreadsheets. This benchmark includes 108 manually sourced or crafted tasks in total, requiring interacting with multiple Apps over around 20 turns on average to complete. Each task is strictly verifiable through dedicated evaluation scripts. Comprehensive evaluation of SOTA models highlights their significant shortcomings: the best-performing model, Claude-4.5-Sonnet, achieves only a 38.6% success rate with 20.2 tool calling turns on average, while the top open-weights model DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp reaches 20.1%. We expect Toolathlon to drive the development of more capable language agents for real-world, long-horizon task execution.

keyInsights: Effective exploration of observations and environment states appears more critical for agentic tasks than increased internal reasoning, as models often perform better by leveraging environmental feedback.
Handling overlong tool outputs (e.g., extensive HTML) and managing long-context information remains a significant challenge for most models, contributing to reduced success rates.

The discrepancy between potential capability (Pass@3) and consistent reliability (Pass^3) highlights that while models may sometimes solve tasks, they often lack the dependability required for real-world application.
originalProblem: Existing language agent benchmarks often exhibit a restricted focus, covering narrow domains or simplified tasks, and lack the diversity, realism, and long-horizon complexity required for practical evaluation.
Prior evaluation methods frequently rely on synthetic environments, simplified initial states, or subjective Language Model (LLM) judges, which can compromise reliability and reproducibility.
Current benchmarks typically do not require agents to orchestrate operations across multiple disparate real-world applications or interpret ambiguous, fuzzy user instructions.
results: State-of-the-art language models show substantial limitations, with the best-performing model, Claude-4.5-Sonnet, achieving only a 38.6% success rate, and the top open-weight model, DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp, reaching 20.1% on TOOLATHLON's tasks.

Proprietary models generally outperformed open-source models, with Claude-4.5-Sonnet demonstrating strong general capabilities and other models showing specialized strengths across different task categories.

Analysis revealed common failure modes including tool execution errors (e.g., incorrect parameter passing), challenges with long-context processing, and premature task termination, indicating key areas for future agent robustness and reliability improvements.
solution: The Tool Decathlon (TOOLATHLON) benchmark was developed with 108 manually sourced or crafted tasks, encompassing 32 real-world software applications and 604 tools, designed for diversity, realism, and long-horizon complexity.
A hybrid environment setup utilizes remote applications and locally containerized open-source software to provide realistic initial states and enable scalable, complex state simulation.

A strictly verifiable, execution-based evaluation framework with unique, manually crafted scripts for each task ensures precise and consistent measurement of success against static or dynamically generated ground-truth states.
paper_summary.summary: The Tool Decathlon (TOOLATHLON) is introduced as a new benchmark for evaluating language agents on diverse, realistic, and long-horizon tasks spanning 32 real-world applications. Evaluations reveal that state-of-the-art models, including Claude-4.5-Sonnet, achieve only up to a 38.6% success rate, underscoring significant limitations in current agent capabilities for complex, multi-step operations.

title: The Tool Decathlon: Benchmarking Language Agents for Diverse, Realistic, and Long-Horizon Task Execution

abstract:
As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as agents, their
integration into interactive environments and tool use introduce new safety
challenges beyond those associated with the models themselves. However, the
absence of comprehensive benchmarks for evaluating agent safety presents a
significant barrier to effective assessment and further improvement. In this
paper, we introduce Agent-SafetyBench, a comprehensive benchmark designed to
evaluate the safety of LLM agents. Agent-SafetyBench encompasses 349
interaction environments and 2,000 test cases, evaluating 8 categories of
safety risks and covering 10 common failure modes frequently encountered in
unsafe interactions. Our evaluation of 16 popular LLM agents reveals a
concerning result: none of the agents achieves a safety score above 60%. This
highlights significant safety challenges in LLM agents and underscores the
considerable need for improvement. Through failure mode and helpfulness
analysis, we summarize two fundamental safety defects in current LLM agents:
lack of robustness and lack of risk awareness. Furthermore, our findings
suggest that reliance on defense prompts alone may be insufficient to address
these safety issues, emphasizing the need for more advanced and robust
strategies. To drive progress in this area, Agent-SafetyBench has been released
at this https URL to facilitate further
research in agent safety evaluation and improvement.

keyInsights: LLM agents exhibit two fundamental safety defects: a pervasive lack of robustness in reliably utilizing tools and insufficient risk awareness regarding potential negative impacts of their actions.

Behavioral safety issues, involving tool use and environmental interaction, are significantly more challenging for LLM agents than content-level safety concerns, even without explicit jailbreak attacks.

Simple defense prompts provide only limited improvements in agent safety, suggesting that prompt engineering alone is insufficient and more advanced mitigation strategies are required to address fundamental vulnerabilities.
originalProblem: Existing LLM safety benchmarks primarily focus on content generation, inadequately addressing the unique behavioral risks introduced by LLM agents interacting with external environments and tools.
Current agent safety benchmarks are limited in interaction environment diversity, comprehensive risk coverage, test case quantity, and systematic failure mode taxonomy.
A robust, comprehensive evaluation framework is needed to identify and address the evolving safety challenges of LLM agents, ensuring responsible development and deployment.
results: None of the 16 evaluated LLM agents achieved a total safety score above 60%, with many performing below 20%, indicating widespread safety deficiencies.
Agents scored an average of only 15.6% in the 'Spread unsafe information / misinformation' risk category, highlighting a high propensity for agents to disseminate harmful content through tools.
Stronger, proprietary agents generally attained higher safety scores, though even the best-performing models remained well below acceptable safety thresholds.
solution: AGENT-SAFETYBENCH was constructed with 349 diverse interactive environments (including novel ones without public APIs), 8 comprehensive safety risk categories, and 2,000 meticulously crafted test cases.
A specialized, fine-tuned local scorer based on Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct was developed, achieving 91.5% accuracy in judging behavioral safety outcomes, outperforming raw GPT-4o.
The benchmark incorporates 10 elaborated failure modes and was rigorously quality-controlled through multiple rounds of manual review, automated validation, and cross-validation for high reliability.
paper_summary.summary: The Conversational AI group at Tsinghua University developed AGENT-SAFETYBENCH, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating the behavioral safety of Large Language Model agents in diverse interactive environments. Evaluations of 16 popular agents revealed none achieved a total safety score above 60%, exposing prevalent vulnerabilities in agent robustness and risk awareness, especially for behavioral safety tasks.

title: Agent-SafetyBench: Evaluating the Safety of LLM Agents

abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world applications requiring complex, long-horizon workflows. However, existing benchmarks predominantly focus on atomic tasks that are self-contained and independent, failing to capture the long-term contextual dependencies and multi-interaction coordination required in realistic scenarios. To address this gap, we introduce OdysseyBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on long-horizon workflows across diverse office applications including Word, Excel, PDF, Email, and Calendar. Our benchmark comprises two complementary splits: OdysseyBench+ with 300 tasks derived from real-world use cases, and OdysseyBench-Neo with 302 newly synthesized complex tasks. Each task requires agent to identify essential information from long-horizon interaction histories and perform multi-step reasoning across various applications. To enable scalable benchmark creation, we propose HomerAgents, a multi-agent framework that automates the generation of long-horizon workflow benchmarks through systematic environment exploration, task generation, and dialogue synthesis. Our extensive evaluation demonstrates that OdysseyBench effectively challenges state-of-the-art LLM agents, providing more accurate assessment of their capabilities in complex, real-world contexts compared to existing atomic task benchmarks. We believe that OdysseyBench will serve as a valuable resource for advancing the development and evaluation of LLM agents in real-world productivity scenarios. In addition, we release OdysseyBench and HomerAgents to foster research along this line.

keyInsights: LLM agent performance degrades substantially as the number of office applications involved in a task increases, highlighting challenges in multi-tool orchestration and state management.

Semantic summarization of dialogue history, particularly at the chunk level, improves agent performance on long-horizon tasks, often surpassing full context provision while using significantly fewer tokens.
The quality and relevance of retrieved context are more critical than the sheer volume, as increasing top-k retrieved documents beyond an optimal point can introduce noise and decrease performance.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks predominantly focus on self-contained, atomic tasks, failing to capture real-world workflow complexity.
There is a lack of evaluation for LLM agents on long-horizon, multi-step tasks requiring sustained interaction, contextual understanding, and coordination across diverse office applications.
Creating such complex benchmarks through human annotation is costly and not scalable, limiting task diversity.
results: State-of-the-art LLM agents exhibited significant performance degradation, with average success rates dropping substantially (e.g., 72.83% to 30.36% for one model) when tasks involved more applications.

Semantic context summarization, especially chunk-level, consistently improved agent performance (e.g., GPT-4o achieved 56.29% on OdysseyBench-Neo) while requiring less than 20% of the tokens compared to long-context baselines.

Error analysis identified common failure patterns including inaccurate planning, incorrect tool calls, and difficulty with file creation or modification across various office applications, particularly `.docx` and `.xlsx`.
solution: Introduced OdysseyBench, a two-split benchmark (OdysseyBench+ and OdysseyBench-Neo) featuring long-horizon, multi-application office workflows spanning Word, Excel, PDF, Email, and Calendar.
Developed HOMERAGENTS, a multi-agent framework utilizing GPT-4.1 to automatically generate diverse and complex long-horizon tasks and their corresponding multi-day dialogue histories.

Implemented and evaluated state-of-the-art LLM agents on OdysseyBench with different context management strategies, including long-context and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) using raw versus semantically summarized context.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduced OdysseyBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate LLM agents on long-horizon, complex workflows across multiple office applications, revealing that current agents face challenges in multi-application coordination but perform better when provided with semantically summarized context. This approach improved agent performance on complex tasks while substantially reducing token requirements.

title: OdysseyBench: Evaluating LLM Agents on Long-Horizon Complex Office Application Workflows

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) based autonomous agents demonstrate multifaceted capabilities to contribute substantially to economic production. However, existing benchmarks remain focused on single agentic capability, failing to capture long-horizon real-world scenarios. Moreover, the reliance on human-in-the-loop feedback for realistic tasks creates a scalability bottleneck, hindering automated rollout collection and evaluation. To bridge this gap, we introduce AgencyBench, a comprehensive benchmark derived from daily AI usage, evaluating 6 core agentic capabilities across 32 real-world scenarios, comprising 138 tasks with specific queries, deliverables, and rubrics. These scenarios require an average of 90 tool calls, 1 million tokens, and hours of execution time to resolve. To enable automated evaluation, we employ a user simulation agent to provide iterative feedback, and a Docker sandbox to conduct visual and functional rubric-based assessment. Experiments reveal that closed-source models significantly outperform open-source models (48.4% vs 32.1%). Further analysis reveals significant disparities across models in resource efficiency, feedback-driven self-correction, and specific tool-use preferences. Finally, we investigate the impact of agentic scaffolds, observing that proprietary models demonstrate superior performance within their native ecosystems (e.g., Claude-4.5-Opus via Claude-Agent-SDK), while open-source models exhibit distinct performance peaks, suggesting potential optimization for specific execution frameworks. AgencyBench serves as a critical testbed for next-generation agents, highlighting the necessity of co-optimizing model architecture with agentic frameworks. We believe this work sheds light on the future direction of autonomous agents, and we release the full benchmark and evaluation toolkit at this https URL.

keyInsights: Proprietary LLM agents significantly outperform open-source counterparts, achieving an average score of 48.4% compared to 32.1% from open-source models on complex real-world tasks.

Agentic performance is not solely an intrinsic model property but is strongly influenced by the coupling between the LLM and its agentic scaffold, as evidenced by 'ecosystem synergy' for proprietary models.

Models exhibit distinct resource consumption patterns and problem-solving strategies, with some prioritizing 'brute-force' reasoning with high token usage (e.g., GPT-5.2) while others optimize for frugality (e.g., Grok-4.1-Fast).
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for autonomous agents often feature short-horizon tasks with limited interaction depth and tool calls, failing to capture real-world complexity.
Current benchmarks tend to focus on single agentic capabilities, inadequately assessing the multifaceted nature of real-world problems that demand a combination of skills.
Many complex agent evaluations still rely on human-in-the-loop feedback, creating scalability bottlenecks and hindering automated, reproducible assessment.
results: GPT-5.2 achieved the highest average score (56.5%) among proprietary models, while GLM-4.6 led open-source models (38.6%), demonstrating varying strengths across capabilities like game development and research.
Top-tier proprietary models showed superior feedback-driven self-correction, with GPT-5.2 exhibiting an 88.9% relative increase in pass rate (Pass@2 vs. Pass@1) after receiving feedback.

AGENCYBENCH tasks proved genuinely long-horizon, requiring an average of 1 million tokens, hours of execution time, and 90 tool calls per scenario, highlighting the demanding nature of real-world agentic challenges.
solution: AGENCYBENCH introduces 138 authentic tasks across 32 diverse real-world scenarios, demanding an average of 1 million tokens and 90 tool calls for resolution, covering six core agentic capabilities.
A unified automated evaluation framework was developed, employing a user simulation agent for iterative feedback and a Docker-based remote sandbox for visual and functional rubric-based assessment.
The benchmark tasks were meticulously constructed by 20 human experts, with an additional panel of four experts conducting a comprehensive review to ensure high quality and accuracy.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduced AGENCYBENCH, a comprehensive benchmark for LLM-based autonomous agents designed for 1M-token real-world contexts, enabling the rigorous evaluation of long-horizon tasks. It revealed a significant performance gap between proprietary (48.4% average score) and open-source (32.1%) models and highlighted varied efficiencies and self-correction capabilities across agents.

title: AgencyBench: Benchmarking the Frontiers of Autonomous Agents in 1M-Token Real-World Contexts

abstract: Autonomous agent systems powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated promising capabilities in automating complex tasks. However, current evaluations largely rely on success rates without systematically analyzing the interactions, communication mechanisms, and failure causes within these systems. To bridge this gap, we present a benchmark of 34 representative programmable tasks designed to rigorously assess autonomous agents. Using this benchmark, we evaluate three popular open-source agent frameworks combined with two LLM backbones, observing a task completion rate of approximately 50%. Through in-depth failure analysis, we develop a three-tier taxonomy of failure causes aligned with task phases, highlighting planning errors, task execution issues, and incorrect response generation. Based on these insights, we propose actionable improvements to enhance agent planning and self-diagnosis capabilities. Our failure taxonomy, together with mitigation advice, provides an empirical foundation for developing more robust and effective autonomous agent systems in the future.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Current autonomous agents achieve an approximate 50% task completion rate, indicating significant room for improvement in real-world complex tasks.
Agent performance varies considerably with task complexity, excelling in structured tasks but struggling with reasoning-intensive ones like web crawling.
Stronger LLMs like GPT-4o can exhibit 'overthinking' issues, where complex planning conflicts with safety constraints, sometimes leading to worse performance than smaller models like GPT-4o mini.

Task failures can be systematically categorized into planning, execution, and response generation issues, with specific causes like improper decomposition, incorrect code, and context window restraints being prevalent.
originalProblem: Existing evaluations of autonomous agent systems primarily focus on simple success rates, overlooking internal dynamics and specific points of failure.
A lack of systematic analysis exists in exploring agent communication, information passing, and the root causes of task failures.
Without understanding fundamental bottlenecks, it is difficult to effectively diagnose problems and design more robust agent systems.
results: Overall task completion rate across evaluated frameworks and LLMs was approximately 50%, with TaskWeaver achieving up to 50% with GPT-4o.

Agents demonstrated higher success rates in structured tasks (e.g., TaskWeaver with GPT-4o reached 67% for Data Analysis, 75% for File Operations) compared to reasoning-intensive tasks like Web Crawling (as low as 17%).

A comprehensive three-tier taxonomy was developed, categorizing 19 distinct failure causes into Task Planning, Task Execution, and Response Generation, derived from the analysis of 104 observed failures.
solution: Developed a benchmark of 34 executable, automatically evaluable, and partially solvable programmable tasks across web crawling, data analysis, and file operations.
Evaluated three popular open-source agent frameworks (TaskWeaver, MetaGPT, AutoGen) using GPT-4o and GPT-4o mini as LLM backbones.
Conducted a manual investigation of 104 task failures from 204 runs to develop a three-tier, 19-cause failure taxonomy spanning Task Planning, Task Execution, and Response Generation.
paper_summary.summary: The paper systematically investigates failures in LLM-powered autonomous agents by introducing a benchmark of 34 programmable tasks and a comprehensive three-tier, 19-cause failure taxonomy. This work quantifies agent performance at approximately 50% success rate and reveals common failure patterns in task planning, execution, and response generation, offering actionable insights for building more robust systems.

title: Exploring Autonomous Agents: A Closer Look at Why They Fail When Completing Tasks

abstract:
We study the use of large language model-based agents for interacting with
software via web browsers. Unlike prior work, we focus on measuring the agents'
ability to perform tasks that span the typical daily work of knowledge workers
utilizing enterprise software systems. To this end, we propose WorkArena, a
remote-hosted benchmark of 33 tasks based on the widely-used ServiceNow
platform. We also introduce BrowserGym, an environment for the design and
evaluation of such agents, offering a rich set of actions as well as multimodal
observations. Our empirical evaluation reveals that while current agents show
promise on WorkArena, there remains a considerable gap towards achieving full
task automation. Notably, our analysis uncovers a significant performance
disparity between open and closed-source LLMs, highlighting a critical area for
future exploration and development in the field.

keyInsights: Chain-of-Thought prompting is crucial for LLM-based web agents, as disabling it severely degraded performance across all evaluated models and benchmarks.

Adding more features or extensive memory mechanisms (e.g., `long description`, `use think history`) did not consistently improve performance for all LLMs and could sometimes degrade it, especially for weaker models with limited context windows, due to prompt length or agents sticking to erroneous decisions.

Current vision-augmented models (GPT-4o-V) provided only minor performance improvements on web tasks, suggesting a need for more screen-related training data to effectively leverage visual information in these contexts.
originalProblem: Existing web agent benchmarks primarily focus on general web browsing, e-commerce, or synthetic environments, neglecting the unique complexities of enterprise software systems.

Enterprise applications often feature non-standard UIs, dynamic elements, nested iFrames, shadow DOMs, and very large Document Object Models (DOMs), leading to repetitive, burdensome tasks for knowledge workers and accessibility challenges.

A lack of standardized, robust environments and benchmarks tailored for real-world enterprise software hinders the development and evaluation of advanced web agents capable of assisting with professional tasks.
results: GPT-4o achieved a 42.7% success rate on the challenging WorkArena benchmark, significantly outperforming GPT-3.5 (6.1%) and Llama3 (17.9%), establishing its current leadership in this domain.

WorkArena proved to be a highly challenging environment; even GPT-4o did not achieve 100% success on any task, with all LLMs failing completely on specific list-based tasks requiring interaction with non-standard HTML widgets.
The GPT-4o agent also demonstrated strong performance on existing benchmarks, achieving 66.1% on MiniWoB and improving upon prior reported scores on WebArena with 23.5%.
solution: The WorkArena benchmark was developed, comprising 33 tasks and over 19,000 unique instances mirroring common knowledge work on the widely-used ServiceNow platform.

BrowserGym was introduced as a generic, robust, and extensible browser environment built on the OpenAI Gym standard, offering rich multimodal observations (HTML, Accessibility Tree, screenshot) and a customizable action space, compatible with existing benchmarks.

An empirical evaluation was conducted using state-of-the-art LLM-based agents (GPT-4o, GPT-3.5, Llama3) with chain-of-thought prompting to assess their ability to solve WorkArena tasks, as well as tasks in MiniWoB and WebArena.
paper_summary.summary: ServiceNow Research, in collaboration with several academic institutions, introduces WorkArena, a benchmark for evaluating LLM-based web agents on complex knowledge work tasks within the ServiceNow enterprise platform, and BrowserGym, a versatile browser environment. Their evaluation showed that GPT-4o achieved a 42.7% success rate on WorkArena, underscoring the challenges posed by real-world enterprise user interfaces for current AI.

title:
WorkArena: How Capable Are Web Agents at Solving Common Knowledge Work
  Tasks?

abstract: Recent advances in AI agents capable of solving complex, everyday tasks, from scheduling to customer service, have enabled deployment in real-world settings, but their possibilities for unsafe behavior demands rigorous evaluation. While prior benchmarks have attempted to assess agent safety, most fall short by relying on simulated environments, narrow task domains, or unrealistic tool abstractions. We introduce OpenAgentSafety, a comprehensive and modular framework for evaluating agent behavior across eight critical risk categories. Unlike prior work, our framework evaluates agents that interact with real tools, including web browsers, code execution environments, file systems, bash shells, and messaging platforms; and supports over 350 multi-turn, multi-user tasks spanning both benign and adversarial user intents. OpenAgentSafety is designed for extensibility, allowing researchers to add tools, tasks, websites, and adversarial strategies with minimal effort. It combines rule-based analysis with LLM-as-judge assessments to detect both overt and subtle unsafe behaviors. Empirical analysis of five prominent LLMs in agentic scenarios reveals unsafe behavior in 51.2% of safety-vulnerable tasks with Claude-Sonnet-3.7, to 72.7% with o3-mini, highlighting critical safety vulnerabilities and the need for stronger safeguards before real-world deployment.

keyInsights: Modern LLM agents, even advanced ones, exhibit pervasive unsafe behavior (49-73% in safety-vulnerable tasks), particularly when confronting systemic risks that require procedural judgment or understanding of institutional norms.

Unsafe behaviors are frequently elicited by benign user prompts or hidden malicious intents from secondary actors (NPCs), demonstrating a fragility in multi-turn intent tracking and the generalization of refusal strategies.

The evaluation revealed that LLM-as-Judge models often underestimate implied unsafe behaviors and overestimate failure rates by misinterpreting superficial errors, highlighting the necessity of hybrid evaluation methods.
originalProblem: Existing LLM safety evaluations primarily focus on static output generation (e.g., bias, toxicity) and do not adequately address the unique safety challenges of AI agents that interact with dynamic environments and execute actions via real-world tools.

Prior benchmarks for LLM agents often rely on simulated environments, simplified tool APIs, or narrow task domains, failing to capture the complexities and risks of realistic, multi-step interactions, particularly those involving multi-turn and multi-user dynamics.

The rapid development and deployment of LLM-powered agents with access to powerful tools have outpaced the progress in robust safety assurance methods, leading to a disparity between agent capabilities and the investigation of potential failure modes.
results: All seven prominent LLMs evaluated exhibited substantial unsafe behavior, with unsafe rates ranging from 49.06% (Claude Sonnet 4) to 72.73% (o3-mini) in safety-vulnerable tasks.

Systemic risk categories (e.g., computer security compromise, legal violations, privacy breaches) consistently showed the highest unsafe rates (72-86%), as agents frequently disregarded authorization policies or institutional norms.

Agents displayed unsafe actions in 50-86% of tasks with benign prompts and showed increased unsafe rates (44.9-69.2%) when malicious goals were introduced by NPCs, indicating challenges in multi-turn intent tracking and refusal strategies.
solution: Introduces OPENAGENTSAFETY (OA-SAFETY), an open-source framework that places AI agents in a containerized sandbox with access to real-world tools (Unix shell, file system, Python interpreter, web browser) and locally hosted replicas of real platforms like GitLab.

Supports diverse user intentions, from benign ambiguity to explicit adversarial manipulation, and incorporates multi-user scenarios by integrating secondary actors (NPCs) with varied or conflicting goals, scaled to over 350 tasks.

Employs a hybrid evaluation strategy combining precise rule-based evaluators to detect specific unsafe environmental outcomes with LLM-as-Judge assessments to identify unsafe intent or partial malicious actions in agent trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Carnegie Mellon University and the Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence developed OPENAGENTSAFETY, an open-source framework for evaluating AI agent safety in realistic, high-risk scenarios using sandboxed real-world tools and complex multi-user interactions. Empirical evaluation revealed that all seven tested LLMs exhibited unsafe behavior in 49-73% of safety-vulnerable tasks, especially when confronting systemic risks or subtle malicious intents from secondary actors.

title: OpenAgentSafety: A Comprehensive Framework for Evaluating Real-World AI Agent Safety

abstract: With the integration of large language models (LLMs), embodied agents have strong capabilities to understand and plan complicated natural language instructions. However, a foreseeable issue is that those embodied agents can also flawlessly execute some hazardous tasks, potentially causing damages in the real world. Existing benchmarks predominantly overlook critical safety risks, focusing solely on planning performance, while a few evaluate LLMs' safety awareness only on non-interactive image-text data. To address this gap, we present SafeAgentBench -- the first comprehensive benchmark for safety-aware task planning of embodied LLM agents in interactive simulation environments, covering both explicit and implicit hazards. SafeAgentBench includes: (1) an executable, diverse, and high-quality dataset of 750 tasks, rigorously curated to cover 10 potential hazards and 3 task types; (2) SafeAgentEnv, a universal embodied environment with a low-level controller, supporting multi-agent execution with 17 high-level actions for 9 state-of-the-art baselines; and (3) reliable evaluation methods from both execution and semantic perspectives. Experimental results show that, although agents based on different design frameworks exhibit substantial differences in task success rates, their overall safety awareness remains weak. The most safety-conscious baseline achieves only a 10% rejection rate for detailed hazardous tasks. Moreover, simply replacing the LLM driving the agent does not lead to notable improvements in safety awareness. Dataset and codes are available in this https URL and this https URL.

keyInsights: State-of-the-art embodied LLM agents demonstrate weak intrinsic safety awareness, executing hazardous tasks with minimal proactive rejection (e.g., highest 10% rejection on detailed hazardous tasks).
Agent architectural design exerts a more significant influence on safety performance than the choice of the underlying LLM backbone.
Simple defense strategies, such as compositional designs or LLM-based safety filters, exhibit limited success and can introduce undesirable trade-offs like over-rejection on safe tasks.
originalProblem: Current benchmarks for embodied LLM agents prioritize task planning performance and completion, overlooking critical safety risks.
Existing LLM safety evaluations are largely confined to text-level analysis or non-interactive environments, failing to capture comprehensive safety in physical interaction.
Powerful embodied LLM agents could indiscriminately execute hazardous commands, posing risks to property and human safety in real-world scenarios.
results: Nine state-of-the-art embodied LLM agents, including those powered by GPT-4 and Gemini-2.5-pro, demonstrated weak proactive safety, with rejection rates for explicit hazardous tasks rarely exceeding 10%.
The choice of LLM backbone showed minimal impact on safety awareness, with less than 3% difference in proactive defense rates across various models, suggesting architectural design is more critical.
KARMA, an agent leveraging memory, achieved a 70% completed-and-safe rate on complex long-horizon tasks with implicit safety requirements, outperforming other baselines significantly.
solution: Introduced SafeAgentBench, a comprehensive framework featuring a dataset of 750 tasks covering 10 harm categories and three task types (detailed, abstract, long-horizon) to evaluate explicit and implicit hazards.
Developed SafeAgentEnv, an interactive simulation environment built on AI2-THOR with a novel low-level controller supporting 17 high-level actions for multi-agent testing.
Proposed robust evaluation methods, including a standard execution evaluator and a GPT-4-based semantic evaluator, to accurately assess task completion and safety.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University and Shanghai AI Laboratory developed SafeAgentBench, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the safety awareness of embodied Large Language Model (LLM) agents in interactive simulations, encompassing both explicit and implicit hazards. Benchmarking leading LLM agents revealed that they exhibit weak intrinsic safety awareness, frequently executing hazardous tasks, and indicated that agent architecture plays a more crucial role in safety performance than the specific LLM used.

title: SafeAgentBench: A Benchmark for Safe Task Planning of Embodied LLM Agents

abstract: AI agents deployed as persistent assistants must maintain correct beliefs as their information environment evolves. In practice, evidence is scattered across heterogeneous sources that often contradict one another, new information can invalidate earlier conclusions, and user preferences surface through corrections rather than explicit instructions. Existing benchmarks largely assume static, single-authority settings and do not evaluate whether agents can keep up with this complexity. We introduce ClawArena, a benchmark for evaluating AI agents in evolving information environments. Each scenario maintains a complete hidden ground truth while exposing the agent only to noisy, partial, and sometimes contradictory traces across multi-channel sessions, workspace files, and staged updates. Evaluation is organized around three coupled challenges: multi-source conflict reasoning, dynamic belief revision, and implicit personalization, whose interactions yield a 14-category question taxonomy. Two question formats, multi-choice (set-selection) and shell-based executable checks, test both reasoning and workspace grounding. The current release contains 64 scenarios across 8 professional domains, totaling 1{,}879 evaluation rounds and 365 dynamic updates. Experiments on five agent frameworks and five language models show that both model capability (15.4% range) and framework design (9.2%) substantially affect performance, that self-evolving skill frameworks can partially close model-capability gaps, and that belief revision difficulty is determined by update design strategy rather than the mere presence of updates. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: The intrinsic capabilities of the base language model have a more dominant impact on overall agent performance (0.154 range) compared to the design of the agent framework (0.092 range).

Self-evolving agent frameworks, exemplified by MetaClaw, can improve performance, especially in execution-oriented tasks and during mid-scenario updates, potentially mitigating some underlying model limitations.

Belief revision difficulty is primarily driven by the strategic design of updates, with concentrated and targeted contradictions leading to greater performance drops than merely a high volume of distributed updates.
originalProblem: Existing AI agent benchmarks operate on simplified assumptions, typically using static environments with a single source of truth, failing to mirror real-world complexities.
Agents struggle with resolving conflicting information originating from multiple heterogeneous sources (e.g., chat, files) and discerning source reliability.
Current evaluation methods do not adequately assess an agent's ability to revise prior beliefs in response to new, evolving information or to learn and apply implicit user preferences over time.
results: MetaClaw, a skill-driven self-evolving framework built on OpenClaw, achieved the highest overall score of 0.603 with GPT-5.1, surpassing OpenClaw by 4.1% and demonstrating superior Execution Evaluation.
Language models showed a clear capability gradient with OpenClaw, from Claude Opus 4.6 (0.735) as the top performer, followed by Sonnet 4.6 (0.708), Haiku 4.5 (0.614), and GPT-5.2 (0.581).

Error analysis revealed that workspace grounding capabilities (executable checks) are largely uncorrelated with abstract reasoning (multi-choice scores), indicating distinct challenges, and model-level biases like narrative anchoring can propagate across frameworks.
solution: ClawArena provides a benchmark of 64 scenarios across 8 professional domains, featuring a hidden ground truth and noisy, partial, and potentially contradictory observable information via multi-channel histories and files.
It integrates evaluation across multi-source conflict reasoning, dynamic belief revision, and implicit personalization, using a 14-category question taxonomy to capture diverse failure modes.

Scenarios are constructed with empirical distributions to ensure authenticity and include both multi-choice set-selection questions and shell-based executable checks for comprehensive assessment of abstract reasoning and workspace grounding.
paper_summary.summary: ClawArena introduces a benchmark for evaluating AI agents in complex, dynamic information environments, focusing on multi-source conflict reasoning, dynamic belief revision, and implicit personalization. Experiments indicate that underlying language model capabilities more significantly impact overall performance (0.154 range) than agent framework design, though sophisticated frameworks like MetaClaw can enhance execution-oriented tasks.

title: ClawArena: Benchmarking AI Agents in Evolving Information Environments

abstract: We introduce WorkBench: a benchmark dataset for evaluating agents' ability to execute tasks in a workplace setting. WorkBench contains a sandbox environment with five databases, 26 tools, and 690 tasks. These tasks represent common business activities, such as sending emails and scheduling meetings. The tasks in WorkBench are challenging as they require planning, tool selection, and often multiple actions. If a task has been successfully executed, one (or more) of the database values may change. The correct outcome for each task is unique and unambiguous, which allows for robust, automated evaluation. We call this key contribution outcome-centric evaluation. We evaluate five existing ReAct agents on WorkBench, finding they successfully complete as few as 3% of tasks (Llama2-70B), and just 43% for the best-performing (GPT-4). We further find that agents' errors can result in the wrong action being taken, such as an email being sent to the wrong person. WorkBench reveals weaknesses in agents' ability to undertake common business activities, raising questions about their use in high-stakes workplace settings. WorkBench is publicly available as a free resource at this https URL.

keyInsights: State-of-the-art LLM agents currently perform poorly on realistic workplace tasks, with GPT-4 achieving only 43% accuracy even when given all tools.
Current agents exhibit a notable propensity for "side effects," making unintended and incorrect modifications to the environment, posing risks for real-world deployment.
The "outcome-centric evaluation" method provides a robust and automated way to assess agents, revealing both task completion and unintended consequences.
originalProblem: A lack of benchmarks existed for evaluating autonomous LLM agents that take complex, multi-step actions (not just retrieval) in realistic workplace scenarios.

Existing evaluation methods for action-taking agents often relied on fragile function call matching or subjective human/LLM assessments, hindering objective comparison and understanding of reliability.
There was limited understanding of the practical efficacy, reliability, and potential for unintended consequences ("side effects") of current autonomous agents in business contexts.
results: GPT-4 (OpenAI) achieved the highest accuracy at 43% on WorkBench's 26-tool setting, with other models like Claude-2 and Mixtral-8x7B performing at 26% and 16% respectively.
Agent performance varied significantly across domains, with GPT-4 achieving 65% accuracy on Calendar tasks but only 23% on CRM tasks, and 40% on multi-domain tasks.
Side effects, defined as unintended modifications to the environment, were common, occurring in 54% of Analytics tasks and 22% of Calendar tasks, highlighting a critical safety concern.
solution: The WorkBench dataset provides a self-contained "sandbox" environment with five simulated business databases (e.g., Calendar, Email, CRM) to model a workplace setting.
It features 690 unique tasks, programmatically generated from 69 human-curated templates, designed to require complex, multi-step, and multi-tool interactions.

A novel "outcome-centric evaluation" method is employed, which automatically assesses agent success by comparing the final state of the simulated environment to a predetermined ground-truth outcome, also identifying side effects.
paper_summary.summary: WorkBench, a new benchmark dataset from Mindsdb and universities including Glasgow and Warwick, evaluates autonomous AI agents in realistic workplace settings. It introduces an "outcome-centric evaluation" methodology, which revealed that even state-of-the-art models like GPT-4 achieve only 43% accuracy on complex tasks and frequently produce undesirable "side effects" in the simulated environment.

title: WorkBench: a Benchmark Dataset for Agents in a Realistic Workplace Setting

abstract: As LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed in real-life scenarios, existing benchmarks fail to capture their inherent complexity of handling extensive information, leveraging diverse resources, and managing dynamic user interactions. To address this gap, we introduce VitaBench, a challenging benchmark that evaluates agents on versatile interactive tasks grounded in real-world settings. Drawing from daily applications in food delivery, in-store consumption, and online travel services, VitaBench presents agents with the most complex life-serving simulation environment to date, comprising 66 tools. Through a framework that eliminates domain-specific policies, we enable flexible composition of these scenarios and tools, yielding 100 cross-scenario tasks (main results) and 300 single-scenario tasks. Each task is derived from multiple real user requests and requires agents to reason across temporal and spatial dimensions, utilize complex tool sets, proactively clarify ambiguous instructions, and track shifting user intent throughout multi-turn conversations. Moreover, we propose a rubric-based sliding window evaluator, enabling robust assessment of diverse solution pathways in complex environments and stochastic interactions. Our comprehensive evaluation reveals that even the most advanced models achieve only 30% success rate on cross-scenario tasks, and less than 50% success rate on others. Overall, we believe VitaBench will serve as a valuable resource for advancing the development of AI agents in practical real-world applications. The code, dataset, and leaderboard are available at this https URL

keyInsights: Agentic task complexity in real-world applications can be formally measured across three dimensions: reasoning, tool use, and dynamic interaction.
Graph-based tool dependency modeling enables autonomous agent behavior and cross-domain reasoning, moving beyond rigid policy adherence.
Robust evaluation of complex, long-horizon, multi-turn trajectories requires a rubric-based sliding window approach that persistently tracks criterion satisfaction.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks operate in simplified, controlled settings, failing to capture the intricate complexities of real-world deployments.
Current evaluation frameworks inadequately consider dynamic user interactions, diverse external resources, and extensive environmental information.
Many benchmarks impose rigid, domain-specific policies, limiting agents' autonomous exploration and reasoning capabilities in dynamic environments.
results: State-of-the-art LLM agents achieve only a 30.0% average success rate on cross-scenario tasks, indicating significant limitations in complex real-world application contexts.
"Thinking" mechanisms (e.g., chain-of-thought) consistently enhance agent effectiveness and efficiency, leading to higher success rates and fewer interaction turns.
Detailed error analysis reveals that reasoning errors account for the majority (61.8%) of failures in complex tasks, followed by tool usage errors (21.1%) and interaction errors (7.9%).
solution: VitaBench features 400 interactive tasks directly derived from real "life-serving applications" (delivery, in-store, online travel) to evaluate agents across reasoning, tool, and interaction complexity.
Tool use is modeled as a graph structure to inherently encode domain rules, enabling autonomous agent exploration across interconnected action spaces without verbose policy documents.
A sophisticated user simulator introduces realistic, dynamic multi-turn interaction challenges through unique profiles, behavioral attributes, and progressive information disclosure.
paper_summary.summary: VitaBench, developed by the Meituan LongCat Team, introduces a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on versatile interactive tasks derived from real-world "life-serving applications." It assesses agents across reasoning, tool use, and interaction complexity, revealing that even state-of-the-art models achieve only a 30.0% average success rate on cross-scenario tasks.

title: VitaBench: Benchmarking LLM Agents with Versatile Interactive Tasks in Real-world Applications

abstract: Personal AI agents like OpenClaw run with elevated privileges on users' local machines, where a single successful prompt injection can leak credentials, redirect financial transactions, or destroy files. This threat goes well beyond conventional text-level jailbreaks, yet existing safety evaluations fall short: most test models in isolated chat settings, rely on synthetic environments, and do not account for how the agent framework itself shapes safety outcomes. We introduce CLAWSAFETY, a benchmark of 120 adversarial test scenarios organized along three dimensions (harm domain, attack vector, and harmful action type) and grounded in realistic, high-privilege professional workspaces spanning software engineering, finance, healthcare, law, and DevOps. Each test case embeds adversarial content in one of three channels the agent encounters during normal work: workspace skill files, emails from trusted senders, and web pages. We evaluate five frontier LLMs as agent backbones, running 2,520 sandboxed trials across all configurations. Attack success rates (ASR) range from 40\% to 75\% across models and vary sharply by injection vector, with skill instructions (highest trust) consistently more dangerous than email or web content. Action-trace analysis reveals that the strongest model maintains hard boundaries against credential forwarding and destructive actions, while weaker models permit both. Cross-scaffold experiments on three agent frameworks further demonstrate that safety is not determined by the backbone model alone but depends on the full deployment stack, calling for safety evaluation that treats model and framework as joint variables. Code and data will be available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Chat-level safety of LLMs does not reliably translate to action-level safety in autonomous agents; models safe in chat can still perform harmful actions via tool calls.
Agent safety is a system-level property influenced not only by the backbone LLM but also significantly by the agent framework's scaffolding, tool orchestration, and memory management.

Attack vectors exhibit a trust-level gradient (skill > email > web injection), with agent defenses often being intent-sensitive (e.g., differentiating imperative vs. declarative phrasing) rather than purely content-sensitive.
originalProblem: Autonomous agents powered by LLMs operate with elevated privileges on user machines, creating a larger attack surface susceptible to indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks that can cause real-world harm.

Prior safety research primarily focused on text-level vulnerabilities and isolated chat environments, failing to quantify the compliance gap between chat-level safety and agent-level action execution.
Existing benchmarks often rely on synthetic environments and do not jointly consider the LLM backbone and agent framework as co-variables, obscuring their combined influence on adversarial risk.
results: Frontier LLMs exhibited overall Attack Success Rates (ASR) ranging from 40% to 75% against indirect prompt injections, with Claude Sonnet 4.6 demonstrating the lowest ASR (40.0%) and GPT-5.1 the highest (75.0%).
A consistent trust-level gradient was observed across injection vectors on OpenClaw, with skill injection achieving the highest ASR (69.4%) and web injection the lowest (38.4%).

Agent scaffold choice alone shifted Claude Sonnet 4.6's overall ASR by 8.6 percentage points (from 40.0% to 48.6%), and reversed the trust-level gradient for email vs. skill injection on different frameworks.
solution: Introduced CLAWSAFETY, a benchmark designed to evaluate personal AI agents against realistic indirect prompt injection threats in high-privilege professional workspaces.
Developed a multi-dimensional threat taxonomy categorizing adversarial scenarios by harm domain, attack vector (skill, email, web injection), and harmful action type.
Employed realistic workspace construction, multi-turn conversation design, and iterative refinement of attack vectors to simulate authentic adversarial conditions.
paper_summary.summary: The CLAWSAFETY benchmark assesses the safety of personal AI agents against indirect prompt injection, revealing that frontier LLMs exhibit 40-75% attack success rates in agentic environments despite chat-level safety. The study shows agent framework choice and attack vector trust levels modulate these vulnerabilities.

title: ClawSafety: "Safe" LLMs, Unsafe Agents

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are evolving into agentic systems that reason, plan, and operate external tools. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a key enabler of this transition, offering a standardized interface for connecting LLMs with heterogeneous tools and services. Yet MCP's openness and multi-server workflows introduce new safety risks that existing benchmarks fail to capture, as they focus on isolated attacks or lack real-world coverage. We present MCP-SafetyBench, a comprehensive benchmark built on real MCP servers that supports realistic multi-turn evaluation across five domains: browser automation, financial analysis, location navigation, repository management, and web search. It incorporates a unified taxonomy of 20 MCP attack types spanning server, host, and user sides, and includes tasks requiring multi-step reasoning and cross-server coordination under uncertainty. Using MCP-SafetyBench, we systematically evaluate leading open- and closed-source LLMs, revealing that all models remain vulnerable to MCP attacks, with a notable safety-utility trade-off. Our results highlight the urgent need for stronger defenses and establish MCP-SafetyBench as a foundation for diagnosing and mitigating safety risks in real-world MCP deployments.
title: MCP-SafetyBench: A Benchmark for Safety Evaluation of Large Language Models with Real-World MCP Servers

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly serving as autonomous agents, and their utilization of external tools via the Model Context Protocol (MCP) is considered a future trend. Current MCP evaluation sets suffer from issues such as reliance on external MCP services and a lack of difficulty awareness. To address these limitations, we propose MCPAgentBench, a benchmark based on real-world MCP definitions designed to evaluate the tool-use capabilities of agents. We construct a dataset containing authentic tasks and simulated MCP tools. The evaluation employs a dynamic sandbox environment that presents agents with candidate tool lists containing distractors, thereby testing their tool selection and discrimination abilities. Furthermore, we introduce comprehensive metrics to measure both task completion rates and execution efficiency. Experiments conducted on various latest mainstream Large Language Models reveal significant performance differences in handling complex, multi-step tool invocations. All code is open-source at Github.

keyInsights: Many LLM agents prioritize task completion over execution efficiency, leading to a substantial decline (over 10 points) in Task Efficiency Finish Score (TEFS) compared to Task Finish Score (TFS).
LLM agents, particularly certain OpenAI models, demonstrate widespread deficiencies in efficiently executing parallel tool calls, with some recording zero TEFS for dual parallel tasks.
Agent performance generally degrades as task complexity increases (more tool invocations) and when more distractor tools are present in the candidate list.
originalProblem: Existing MCP tool-use benchmarks suffer from instability and lack reproducibility due to reliance on remote, live MCP servers.
Current benchmarks offer insufficient granularity in task difficulty assessment and often lack metrics for evaluating execution efficiency.
Evaluation frameworks do not systematically test LLM agents' robustness in selecting correct tools when presented with distractors.
results: Claude Sonnet 4.5 exhibited strong performance across TFS, TEFS, and Time Efficiency, often leveraging an aggressive parallelization strategy.
Most LLM agents showed a consistent drop of over 10 points in TEFS compared to TFS, indicating a trade-off between task completion and efficient execution.
OpenAI models demonstrated an "extreme serial approach," leading to zero TEFS scores for Dual Parallel tasks, highlighting a significant challenge in handling parallel tool invocations.
solution: Developed an automated evaluation framework using Autogen with local MCP server deployment, ensuring stable and reproducible testing.
Curated a dataset of 178 authentic tasks and thousands of MCP tools, utilizing LLM-assisted annotation and manual curation.

Introduced a dynamic sandbox environment to test agent robustness with distractor tools and defined new metrics for task completion, execution efficiency (TEFS), token efficiency, and time efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: Peking University researchers developed MCPAgentBench, a new benchmark for evaluating large language model (LLM) agents' use of Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools, providing a stable, reproducible evaluation environment with local server deployment. It introduces metrics for task efficiency and agent robustness to distractors, revealing a consistent performance drop of over 10 points for most models when strict efficiency (TEFS) is considered compared to mere task completion (TFS).

title: MCPAgentBench: A Real-world Task Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Agent MCP Tool Use

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have exhibited great potential in autonomously completing tasks across real-world applications. Despite this, these LLM agents introduce unexpected safety risks when operating in interactive environments. Instead of centering on the harmlessness of LLM-generated content in most prior studies, this work addresses the imperative need for benchmarking the behavioral safety of LLM agents within diverse environments. We introduce R-Judge, a benchmark crafted to evaluate the proficiency of LLMs in judging and identifying safety risks given agent interaction records. R-Judge comprises 569 records of multi-turn agent interaction, encompassing 27 key risk scenarios among 5 application categories and 10 risk types. It is of high-quality curation with annotated safety labels and risk descriptions. Evaluation of 11 LLMs on R-Judge shows considerable room for enhancing the risk awareness of LLMs: The best-performing model, GPT-4o, achieves 74.42% while no other models significantly exceed the random. Moreover, we reveal that risk awareness in open agent scenarios is a multi-dimensional capability involving knowledge and reasoning, thus challenging for LLMs. With further experiments, we find that fine-tuning on safety judgment significantly improve model performance while straightforward prompting mechanisms fail. R-Judge is publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Current large language models (LLMs) generally lack robust behavioral safety awareness, with most models performing unsatisfactorily on the R-Judge benchmark.
Simple prompting mechanisms, such as few-shot chain-of-thought or explicit risk type hints, are largely ineffective for significantly improving LLMs' risk awareness in dynamic agent scenarios.
Targeted fine-tuning on safety judgment tasks shows promising results, with specialized models demonstrating notable gains in behavioral safety capabilities.
originalProblem: LLM agents, when interacting autonomously in real-world environments, can inadvertently or maliciously cause tangible harm (e.g., privacy leakage, data loss) beyond just generating unsafe content.
There was a lack of standardized benchmarks to evaluate an LLM's inherent awareness and proficiency in judging and identifying safety risks from actual multi-turn agent interaction records.
Existing LLM safety research primarily focused on 'content safety' (preventing toxic or biased text), rather than 'behavioral safety' in dynamic, interactive scenarios.
results: R-Judge proved to be a challenging benchmark; most evaluated LLMs performed below or just slightly above random baselines in safety judgment.
GPT-4o emerged as the best-performing model, achieving an F1 score of 74.45% on the overall safety judgment test, though still with significant room for improvement.
Meta-Llama-Guard-2-8B, a fine-tuned safety model, achieved an F1 score of 71.84% (with risk types in prompt), indicating that specialized training significantly enhances risk awareness.
solution: R-Judge formally defines the 'Agent Safety Monitor' task, requiring an LLM to analyze agent interaction records and output both a textual risk analysis and a binary safe/unsafe judgment.

A comprehensive benchmark dataset, R-Judge, was constructed with 569 human-annotated multi-turn agent interaction cases, covering 5 application categories and 10 distinct risk types, including both intended attacks and unintended risks.

A two-pronged evaluation approach was designed, combining quantitative label scores (F1, Recall, Specificity) for safety judgments and qualitative pairwise evaluation using GPT-4 for the effectiveness of risk identification analyses.
paper_summary.summary: R-Judge introduces a benchmark to evaluate large language models' (LLMs) ability to identify and judge safety risks in multi-turn agent interactions. The work establishes that current LLMs, including leading models, demonstrate considerable limitations in behavioral safety awareness, while targeted fine-tuning significantly improves performance.

title: R-Judge: Benchmarking Safety Risk Awareness for LLM Agents

abstract: Large language models are shifting from passive information providers to active agents intended for complex workflows. However, their deployment as reliable AI workers in enterprise is stalled by benchmarks that fail to capture the intricacies of professional environments, specifically, the need for long-horizon planning amidst persistent state changes and strict access protocols. In this work, we introduce EnterpriseOps-Gym, a benchmark designed to evaluate agentic planning in realistic enterprise settings. Specifically, EnterpriseOps-Gym features a containerized sandbox with 164 database tables and 512 functional tools to mimic real-world search friction. Within this environment, agents are evaluated on 1,150 expert-curated tasks across eight mission-critical verticals (including Customer Service, HR, and IT). Our evaluation of 14 frontier models reveals critical limitations in state-of-the-art models: the top-performing Claude Opus 4.5 achieves only 37.4% success. Further analysis shows that providing oracle human plans improves performance by 14-35 percentage points, pinpointing strategic reasoning as the primary bottleneck. Additionally, agents frequently fail to refuse infeasible tasks (best model achieves 53.9%), leading to unintended and potentially harmful side effects. Our findings underscore that current agents are not yet ready for autonomous enterprise deployment. More broadly, EnterpriseOps-Gym provides a concrete testbed to advance the robustness of agentic planning in professional workflows.
