keyInsights: Current frontier LLM agents are not yet sufficiently reliable for autonomous enterprise deployment, with the best model achieving only 37.4% task completion even with oracle tool access.
Strategic planning, particularly constraint-aware plan generation, is the primary bottleneck for agent performance, rather than proficiency in tool invocation or discovery.
Models struggle critically with permission and process compliance and with reliably detecting and refusing infeasible tasks, indicating substantial safety gaps for production environments.
originalProblem: Existing general-purpose tool-use benchmarks treat tool calls as atomic and stateless, lacking the multi-system, policy-constrained, and stateful features of enterprise environments.

Prior single-platform enterprise benchmarks are confined to single vendor ecosystems with shallow database complexity, limited tools, and short task horizons, failing to reflect real-world scale and interconnectedness.
LLM agents struggle with maintaining state coherence in long-horizon planning, adhering to strict enterprise policies, and safely refusing infeasible tasks without corrupting system state.
Current LLM deployment in enterprise settings is impeded by existing benchmarks that inadequately capture the complexities inherent to enterprise workflows.
results: Claude Opus 4.5 achieved the highest average task completion rate at 37.4%, with performance sharply declining in policy-governed domains (28.5% in ITSM) and as task horizon increased.

Adding distractor tools had a negligible impact on performance, suggesting planning, not tool selection, is the primary bottleneck, while increasing thinking budget improved results but plateaued in some domains.
Models correctly abstained from infeasible tasks only around 53.9% of the time, and struggled most with Permission and Process Compliance checks, highlighting weaknesses in policy adherence.
solution: Introduced ENTERPRISEOPS-GYM, a benchmark with a containerized Docker sandbox environment simulating real enterprise complexity, featuring 164 database tables and 512 functional tools across 8 interconnected domains.
Developed 1,150 expert-curated tasks, including 30 intentionally infeasible scenarios, with outcome-based SQL verification scripts to ensure goal achievement, state integrity, and policy compliance.
Evaluated 14 frontier LLMs using a ReAct-style reasoning loop, primarily with oracle tool access, measuring pass@1 task completion and fine-grained verifier-level success rates.
paper_summary.summary: ServiceNow Research developed ENTERPRISEOPS-GYM, a benchmark and high-fidelity simulation for evaluating large language model agents in complex, stateful enterprise environments with multi-system interactions and strict policy constraints. Frontier models achieved only up to 37.4% task completion, revealing planning and policy adherence as major bottlenecks and identifying significant safety gaps in refusing infeasible tasks.

title: EnterpriseOps-Gym: Environments and Evaluations for Stateful Agentic Planning and Tool Use in Enterprise Settings

abstract: Although large language model (LLM)-based agents, exemplified by OpenClaw, are increasingly evolving from task-oriented systems into personalized AI assistants for solving complex real-world tasks, their practical deployment also introduces severe security risks. However, existing agent security research and evaluation frameworks primarily focus on synthetic or task-centric settings, and thus fail to accurately capture the attack surface and risk propagation mechanisms of personalized agents in real-world deployments. To address this gap, we propose Personalized Agent Security Bench (PASB), an end-to-end security evaluation framework tailored for real-world personalized agents. Building upon existing agent attack paradigms, PASB incorporates personalized usage scenarios, realistic toolchains, and long-horizon interactions, enabling black-box, end-to-end security evaluation on real systems. Using OpenClaw as a representative case study, we systematically evaluate its security across multiple personalized scenarios, tool capabilities, and attack types. Our results indicate that OpenClaw exhibits critical vulnerabilities at different execution stages, including user prompt processing, tool usage, and memory retrieval, highlighting substantial security risks in personalized agent deployments. The code for the proposed PASB framework is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Data Science & Artificial Intelligence Research Institute, China Unicom

keyInsights: Vulnerabilities in personalized LLM agents are not isolated, but instead propagate across different execution stages (prompt processing, tool invocation, memory retrieval) and accumulate over long-horizon interactions.
Current prompt-level defenses (e.g., Delimiter, Sandwich) and instruction-based memory protections are insufficient, as residual attack success rates persist even with these applied safeguards.

Effective security evaluation for personalized agents requires a shift from text-level analysis to action-chain and system-level assessment to accurately capture risks in deployed, continuously operating AI systems.
originalProblem: Existing security evaluation frameworks for LLM agents are inadequate for real-world personalized deployments, being primarily tailored for synthetic or task-centric settings.
Personalized AI agents, characterized by persistent operation, accumulation of private user context, and access to high-privilege tools, introduce severe and unaddressed security risks.
Security failures in this new paradigm can lead to tangible harms like unsafe actions and private asset exfiltration, necessitating a comprehensive, system-level security assessment.
results: OpenClaw demonstrated pervasive vulnerabilities, with indirect prompt injection and tool-return attacks achieving Attack Success Rates (ASRs) up to 66.8% without defenses.
Despite the application of prompt-layer defenses, a non-trivial residual ASR persisted across all models and attack types, ranging from 10.5% to 22.0%, indicating continued exposure.

Long-Term Memory extraction and modification were feasible (e.g., 62.5% LTM extraction, 71.5% LTM modification for Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct without defenses), highlighting significant memory-related risks.
solution: The research proposes Personalized Agent Security Bench (PASB), an end-to-end security evaluation framework specifically designed for real-world personalized LLM agents.

PASB formalizes attack tasks by focusing on observable execution traces, including tool calls and their arguments, and defines attack success based on system-level harm predicates like information leakage, unsafe actions, and persistent behaviors.

The framework enables black-box, end-to-end security evaluation in realistic personalized scenarios (external content, memory management, tool interaction risks) using deployed systems such as OpenClaw.
paper_summary.summary: Personalized Agent Security Bench (PASB) is introduced as a framework for evaluating the security of real-world, personalized Large Language Model (LLM) agents. Its evaluation of OpenClaw, a representative personalized agent, found pervasive vulnerabilities where attacks propagated across execution stages and persisted over long interactions, leading to unauthorized actions and data exfiltration, even when existing defenses were applied.

title: From Assistant to Double Agent: Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks on OpenClaw for Personalized Local AI Agent

abstract: Modern Large Language Model (LLM) agents promise end to end assistance with real-world software tasks, yet existing benchmarks evaluate LLM agents almost exclusively in pre-baked environments where every dependency is pre-installed. To fill this gap, we introduce SetupBench, a 93 instance benchmark that isolates the environment-bootstrap skill: starting from a bare Linux sandbox, an agent must install packages, resolve dependency conflicts, initialize databases, and configure background services. Our tasks span seven language ecosystems, five database engines, and multi-service orchestration scenarios, each accompanies by a natural language problem statement and a deterministic success command. Through evaluation of OpenHands, a state-of-the-art coding agent, we find low success rates across task categories, with particular challenges in repository setup (38.9-57.4%) and local database configuration (20.0-53.3%). Our analysis reveals systematic failure modes including incomplete development tooling installation, hallucinated task constraints, and non-persistent environment modifications that break agent-human collaboration workflows. We identify substantial inefficiencies in agent exploration strategies, with 38-89% of actions being unnecessary compared to optimal human behavior. These findings highlight gaps in current agents' practical environment-bootstrap capabilities. By targeting this critical yet under-evaluated capability, SetupBench provides a rigorous yard-stick for the next generation of software developer agents aiming to solve end to end real-wold tasks.

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art LLM agents consistently struggle with practical environment setup, revealing a significant gap between their coding abilities and real-world functional deployment.
Systematic failure modes include neglecting to install test tooling, hallucinating non-existent task constraints, and failing to persist environment changes across shell sessions.

Agents exhibit substantial operational inefficiencies, with 38-69% of their actions being unnecessary compared to optimal human trajectories, indicating limitations in their planning and contextual exploration strategies.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for LLM-based software engineering agents primarily evaluate them in "pre-baked" environments, bypassing crucial initial setup steps like dependency installation and service configuration.
Developers consistently report environment setup, dependency management, and build/configuration issues as major sources of frustration in real-world software development.
There was a lack of a comprehensive, runnable benchmark capable of rigorously assessing an agent's "environment-bootstrap skill" from a minimal system state across diverse technical stacks.
results: Evaluated OpenHands agent variants achieved modest overall success rates on SetupBench, ranging from 34.4% (GPT-4o) to 62.4% (Claude 4).
Agents showed particular difficulty with Local Database Setup (20.0-53.3% success) and Repo Setup (38.9-57.4% success).
Efficiency analysis revealed that 38-69% of agent steps were wasteful, primarily due to redundant file reads, poor instruction following, and irrelevant file access.
solution: Introduces SetupBench, a benchmark with 93 instances spanning repo setup, dependency resolution, database setup, and background service orchestration, designed to be run from a minimal Linux sandbox.
Employs deterministic, single-line validation commands for clear success/failure assessment, ensuring reproducibility and eliminating subjective evaluation.
Covers a wide range of complexities across seven programming languages, five database engines, and various package managers to reflect diverse real-world development scenarios.
paper_summary.summary: SetupBench is a new benchmark developed by Microsoft to evaluate software engineering agents' ability to autonomously set up development environments. It exposes that state-of-the-art agents achieve success rates between 34.4% and 62.4% and frequently fail due to issues like ignoring test tooling, hallucinating constraints, and lacking environmental persistence.

title: SetupBench: Assessing Software Engineering Agents' Ability to Bootstrap Development Environments

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly capable of carrying out long-running, real-world tasks. However, as the amount of context grows, their reliability often deteriorates, a phenomenon known as "context rot". Existing long-context benchmarks primarily focus on single-step settings that evaluate a model's ability to retrieve information from a long snippet. In realistic scenarios, however, LLMs often need to act as agents that explore environments, follow instructions and plans, extract useful information, and predict correct actions under a dynamically growing context. To assess language agents in such settings, we introduce LOCA-bench (a benchmark for LOng-Context Agents). Given a task prompt, LOCA-bench leverages automated and scalable control of environment states to regulate the agent's context length. This design enables LOCA-bench to extend the context length potentially to infinity in a controlled way while keeping the underlying task semantics fixed. LOCA-bench evaluates language agents as a combination of models and scaffolds, including various context management strategies. While agent performance generally degrades as the environment states grow more complex, advanced context management techniques can substantially improve the overall success rate. We open-source LOCA-bench to provide a platform for evaluating models and scaffolds in long-context, agentic scenarios: this https URL

keyInsights: Context rot is a universal issue, causing a sharp decline in language agent performance across all tested models as context length increases, with frontier models maintaining a 2-3x accuracy advantage over open-source models in longer contexts.

Agent exploration behaviors, such as trajectory length and tool calls, tend to plateau at higher context lengths, indicating a cessation of active information gathering despite increasing environmental complexity.

Advanced context engineering strategies, particularly programmatic tool calling, are more effective than simple context editing, offering significant improvements in accuracy by reducing context consumption and providing structured execution.
originalProblem: Existing long-context benchmarks primarily evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) in static, single-step settings, failing to capture the dynamic and interactive nature of real-world agentic tasks.
LLMs consistently suffer from 'context rot,' where their performance and reliability decline as the amount of contextual information grows in complex, long-running tasks.
There is a lack of systematic methods to isolate and quantify the impact of context length on language agent performance while keeping task semantics constant.
results: All evaluated models showed a consistent and sharp decline in average task accuracy as the Environment Description Length (EDL) increased, with performance at 256K EDL being substantially lower than at 8K EDL.

Programmatic tool calling consistently improved accuracy across all models while simultaneously reducing trajectory length, providing up to 10-20% accuracy gains for frontier models like Claude-4.5-Opus.

Detailed analysis identified key failure modes in long contexts, including declining complex reasoning, weaker instruction following, insufficient exploration, and hallucination-like inconsistencies in factual retention.
solution: LOCA-bench was developed using local, database-backed mock servers to simulate diverse real-world services and automatically generate scalable environment states with adjustable complexity.
The benchmark measures context length using Environment Description Length (EDL), allowing for controllable scaling of task context from 8K to 256K tokens across 15 high-quality agentic tasks.

An extensible evaluation platform, built on GEM (Gym for Agentic LLMs), supports the assessment of various LLMs and context engineering strategies (e.g., context editing, memory tools, programmatic tool calling) through execution-based and verifiable outcomes.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Hong Kong University of Science and Technology introduced LOCA-bench, a new benchmark designed to evaluate language agents in dynamic, long-horizon tasks under controllable and extreme context growth. This benchmark quantitatively demonstrates significant performance degradation (context rot) across various models as context length increases and reveals that advanced context engineering strategies, particularly programmatic tool calling, improve agent accuracy and efficiency.

title: LOCA-bench: Benchmarking Language Agents Under Controllable and Extreme Context Growth

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities as autonomous agents through tool use, planning, and decision-making abilities, leading to their widespread adoption across diverse tasks. As task complexity grows, multi-agent LLM systems are increasingly used to solve problems collaboratively. However, safety and security of these systems remains largely under-explored. Existing benchmarks and datasets predominantly focus on single-agent settings, failing to capture the unique vulnerabilities of multi-agent dynamics and co-ordination. To address this gap, we introduce $\textbf{T}$hreats and $\textbf{A}$ttacks in $\textbf{M}$ulti-$\textbf{A}$gent $\textbf{S}$ystems ($\textbf{TAMAS}$), a benchmark designed to evaluate the robustness and safety of multi-agent LLM systems. TAMAS includes five distinct scenarios comprising 300 adversarial instances across six attack types and 211 tools, along with 100 harmless tasks. We assess system performance across ten backbone LLMs and three agent interaction configurations from Autogen and CrewAI frameworks, highlighting critical challenges and failure modes in current multi-agent deployments. Furthermore, we introduce Effective Robustness Score (ERS) to assess the tradeoff between safety and task effectiveness of these frameworks. Our findings show that multi-agent systems are highly vulnerable to adversarial attacks, underscoring the urgent need for stronger defenses. TAMAS provides a foundation for systematically studying and improving the safety of multi-agent LLM systems.

organization_info.name: International Institute of Information Technology, Hyderabad

keyInsights: Multi-agent LLM systems are highly vulnerable to adversarial attacks, with prompt-level attacks like impersonation and direct prompt injection consistently most effective and model-agnostic.
Architectural choices significantly influence vulnerability, with CrewAI's decentralized sequential setup showing improved safety compared to some orchestrator-based configurations.
Agents often comply with explicitly malicious tasks, even when recognizing their harmful nature, indicating a critical failure in current safety mechanisms.
originalProblem: Existing safety benchmarks primarily focus on single-agent LLM systems, neglecting unique vulnerabilities arising from multi-agent interactions.
Multi-agent LLM systems are increasingly deployed in high-stakes environments, yet their security and robustness against emergent threats remain largely unexplored.
The lack of a comprehensive benchmark hinders systematic assessment and comparison of safety across different multi-agent LLM architectures and models.
results: Impersonation and Direct Prompt Injection attacks achieved success rates up to 82% and 81% respectively, across various LLM backbones and frameworks.
Closed-source models exhibited greater resilience to Indirect Prompt Injection attacks compared to open-source models (average successful attack rate of 15.6% vs. 39.2% in centralized setups).
GPT models consistently achieved high Effective Robustness Scores (ERS), demonstrating a strong balance between safety under attack and utility for benign tasks.
solution: Introduced TAMAS, the first benchmark for multi-agent LLM systems, covering five real-world domains (News, Education, Finance, Healthcare, Legal).
Incorporated six distinct adversarial attack types, including multi-agent-specific risks like Byzantine, colluding, and contradicting agents.
Evaluated system safety across two multi-agent frameworks (Autogen, CrewAI), three interaction configurations, and ten diverse LLM backbones.
Developed the 'Effective Robustness Score (ERS)' to quantify the trade-off between system safety under attack and utility for benign tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from IIIT-Hyderabad and Microsoft Research developed TAMAS, the first benchmark for evaluating adversarial risks in multi-agent LLM systems across five high-impact domains. The study found these systems are highly vulnerable to various attacks, with impersonation and direct prompt injection achieving success rates up to 82%, underscoring critical safety challenges.

title: TAMAS: Benchmarking Adversarial Risks in Multi-Agent LLM Systems

abstract: LLM agents are increasingly expected to function as general-purpose systems capable of resolving open-ended user requests. While existing benchmarks focus on domain-aware environments for developing specialized agents, evaluating general-purpose agents requires more realistic settings that challenge them to operate across multiple skills and tools within a unified environment. We introduce General AgentBench, a benchmark that provides such a unified framework for evaluating general LLM agents across search, coding, reasoning, and tool-use domains. Using General AgentBench, we systematically study test-time scaling behaviors under sequential scaling (iterative interaction) and parallel scaling (sampling multiple trajectories). Evaluation of ten leading LLM agents reveals a substantial performance degradation when moving from domain-specific evaluations to this general-agent setting. Moreover, we find that neither scaling methodology yields effective performance improvements in practice, due to two fundamental limitations: context ceiling in sequential scaling and verification gap in parallel scaling. Code is publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: LLM agents experience substantial performance degradation when evaluated in a general, unified multi-domain setting compared to their performance on isolated, domain-specific benchmarks.

Sequential test-time scaling encounters a "context ceiling," where increasing interaction turns beyond a certain point does not consistently improve performance and can lead to stagnation or degradation.

Parallel test-time scaling is hindered by a persistent "verification gap," as agents struggle to reliably identify the best solution from multiple generated trajectories, limiting practical gains despite increased theoretical upper bounds.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks are typically domain-specific, failing to capture the complexity and versatility required for real-world, open-ended tasks.
These specialized evaluations may overestimate agent robustness and generalizability when deployed in multi-domain scenarios.
The effectiveness of test-time scaling strategies, both sequential and parallel, for general-purpose, interactive LLM agents in diverse environments remained largely uncharacterized.
results: Most evaluated LLMs showed an average relative performance drop of 10% to 30% when tested on General AgentBench compared to their domain-specific configurations, with some models experiencing over 60% degradation in certain domains.

Some models demonstrated performance gains in specific domains (e.g., Search) under the general-agent setting due to effective cross-domain tool usage, repurposing specialized tools beyond their primary functions.

While the theoretical upper bound (Pass@K) for parallel scaling improved by approximately 50% from K=1 to K=4, the practical self-choice accuracy for selecting the best trajectory often saturated or decreased, indicating poor self-evaluation capabilities.
solution: Developed General AgentBench, a novel benchmark that unifies tasks from coding, search, tool-use, and reasoning into a single environment with a shared tool interface using the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

Systematically investigated sequential test-time scaling by extending interaction history and parallel test-time scaling by sampling multiple solution trajectories, evaluating both theoretical potential (Pass@K) and practical self-selection ability.
Evaluated ten leading LLMs, including both open-source and proprietary models, to assess their performance and scaling behaviors on the new general-purpose benchmark.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduced General AgentBench, a unified benchmark evaluating large language model agents in realistic, multi-domain environments that integrate diverse tools. Their study found that current agents experience performance degradation in general settings, and test-time scaling strategies are limited by a "context ceiling" in sequential interactions and a "verification gap" when evaluating multiple generated solutions.

title: Benchmark Test-Time Scaling of General LLM Agents

abstract: With the rapid development of multimodal large language models (MLLMs), they are increasingly deployed as autonomous computer-use agents capable of accomplishing complex computer tasks. However, a pressing issue arises: Can the safety risk principles designed and aligned for general MLLMs in dialogue scenarios be effectively transferred to real-world computer-use scenarios? Existing research on evaluating the safety risks of MLLM-based computer-use agents suffers from several limitations: it either lacks realistic interactive environments, or narrowly focuses on one or a few specific risk types. These limitations ignore the complexity, variability, and diversity of real-world environments, thereby restricting comprehensive risk evaluation for computer-use agents. To this end, we introduce \textbf{RiOSWorld}, a benchmark designed to evaluate the potential risks of MLLM-based agents during real-world computer manipulations. Our benchmark includes 492 risky tasks spanning various computer applications, involving web, social media, multimedia, os, email, and office software. We categorize these risks into two major classes based on their risk source: (i) User-originated risks and (ii) Environmental risks. For the evaluation, we evaluate safety risks from two perspectives: (i) Risk goal intention and (ii) Risk goal completion. Extensive experiments with multimodal agents on \textbf{RiOSWorld} demonstrate that current computer-use agents confront significant safety risks in real-world scenarios. Our findings highlight the necessity and urgency of safety alignment for computer-use agents in real-world computer manipulation, providing valuable insights for developing trustworthy computer-use agents. Our benchmark is publicly available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Current MLLM-based agents exhibit a profound and widespread lack of safety awareness and trustworthiness when performing real-world computer operations.
Safety alignment achieved in dialogue-based MLLMs does not effectively generalize to the dynamic and complex environment of direct computer control, indicating a significant 'intrinsic gap'.

Agents are highly susceptible to both external environmental threats (like phishing websites and induced text) and malicious or misguided user instructions (such as executing destructive OS commands or creating harmful content).
originalProblem: Autonomous agents powered by MLLMs are increasingly operating real-world computer environments, but their safety and trustworthiness in these dynamic settings are not well understood.

Existing safety evaluations for MLLM-based agents are limited, often relying on simplified environments, QA formats, or focusing on only a narrow set of risk types, thus failing to capture comprehensive real-world vulnerabilities.
Safety alignment strategies developed for dialogue-based MLLMs do not adequately transfer to the complexities and dynamic nature of real-world computer manipulation.
results: Across all 492 tasks, MLLM-based agents demonstrated a total average unsafe rate of 84.93% for risk goal intention and 59.64% for risk goal completion.

Environmental risks like Phishing Web (99.2% intention / 83.7% completion) and Induced Text (89.8% intention / 88.9% completion) showed extremely high unsafe rates, indicating agents frequently fail to verify authenticity or validate information.

User-originated risks, including Web (95.7% intention / 86.6% completion for downloading pirated software) and OS Operation (91.3% intention / 84.0% completion for destructive commands), highlighted agents' lack of ethical awareness and security protocols.
solution: RiOSWorld provides a comprehensive benchmark built on a realistic Virtual Machine (VM) environment, modeling agent-environment interactions as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP).

It features 492 risky tasks across diverse common computer applications, meticulously categorized into 13 subcategories of environmental (e.g., phishing, pop-ups) and user-originated risks (e.g., downloading pirated software, OS operations).

The benchmark incorporates dynamic threat deployments using Flask applications to simulate real-time, interactive risks, and evaluates safety using both LLM-as-a-judge for risk intention and rule-based evaluators for risk completion.
paper_summary.summary: RiOSWorld is introduced as a novel and comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the safety risks of Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM)-based computer-use agents. The benchmark reveals that current MLLM agents exhibit an average unsafe rate of 84.93% for risk intention and 59.64% for risk completion across a wide array of realistic tasks, highlighting significant vulnerabilities in real-world computer manipulation.

title: RiOSWorld: Benchmarking the Risk of Multimodal Computer-Use Agents

abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents increasingly rely on tool use to complete real-world tasks. While existing works evaluate the LLMs' tool use capability, they largely focus on the final answers yet overlook the detailed tool usage trajectory, i.e., whether tools are selected, parameterized, and ordered correctly. We introduce TRAJECT-Bench, a trajectory-aware benchmark to comprehensively evaluate LLMs' tool use capability through diverse tasks with fine-grained evaluation metrics. TRAJECT-Bench pairs high-fidelity, executable tools across practical domains with tasks grounded in production-style APIs, and synthesizes trajectories that vary in breadth (parallel calls) and depth (interdependent chains). Besides final accuracy, TRAJECT-Bench also reports trajectory-level diagnostics, including tool selection and argument correctness, and dependency/order satisfaction. Analyses reveal failure modes such as similar tool confusion and parameter-blind selection, and scaling behavior with tool diversity and trajectory length where the bottleneck of transiting from short to mid-length trajectories is revealed, offering actionable guidance for LLMs' tool use.

keyInsights: LLMs face substantial challenges in interpreting complex user intents from indirect queries and in scaling their tool-use capabilities for longer, more numerous tool sequences.
Current retrieval-augmented generation methods for tool selection often prove insufficient for complex, implicit queries, struggling to retrieve necessary tools.

Agentic frameworks like ReAct and dynamic tool retrieval modestly improve tool-use performance, yet fundamental issues in precise tool selection and parameterization persist across state-of-the-art models.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for LLM tool use lack diverse and complex tool-use trajectories, often focusing on short or simulated interactions.
Evaluation queries in current benchmarks are typically straightforward, failing to assess LLMs' ability to infer intent from naturalistic, indirect language.
Prior evaluation schemes over-rely on final-answer metrics, which obscure specific tool selection, parameterization, and sequencing errors, hindering diagnostic analysis.
results: LLM performance shows a substantial drop on "hard" (indirect) queries compared to "simple" ones (e.g., Claude-4 Exact Match dropped from 0.846 to 0.445).
Tool-use performance degrades consistently as the number of tools in a trajectory increases, with a notable bottleneck observed when scaling from 3 to 5 tools.
The ReAct framework consistently improved Exact Match (e.g., Claude-4 on parallel hard queries from 0.445 to 0.463), with dynamic retrieval further boosting it (to 0.473 for Claude-4).
solution: Introduces TRAJECT-Bench, a benchmark featuring over 1,000 high-fidelity, executable tools from diverse real-world domains.
Synthesizes complex tool-use trajectories, including parallel and sequential structures, varying from 3 to 10+ tools.
Provides dual-difficulty user queries (simple and hard) for each trajectory, coupled with fine-grained, trajectory-aware evaluation metrics like Exact Match and Tool Usage.
paper_summary.summary: A new benchmark called TRAJECT-Bench introduces a rigorous framework for evaluating Large Language Model (LLM) agents' tool-use capabilities using over 1,000 real-world APIs, complex multi-step trajectories, and both direct and indirect user queries. Evaluations reveal LLMs struggle significantly with inferring intent from indirect queries and scaling tool use, though agentic frameworks like ReAct provide modest improvements.

title: TRAJECT-Bench:A Trajectory-Aware Benchmark for Evaluating Agentic Tool Use

abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have significantly impacted data science workflows, giving rise to specialized data science agents designed to automate analytical tasks. Despite rapid adoption, systematic benchmarks evaluating the efficacy and limitations of these agents remain scarce. In this paper, we introduce a comprehensive benchmark specifically crafted to reflect real-world user interactions with data science agents by observing usage of our commercial applications. We evaluate three LLMs: Claude-4.0-Sonnet, Gemini-2.5-Flash, and OpenAI-o4-Mini across three approaches: zero-shot with context engineering, multi-step with context engineering, and with SmolAgent. Our benchmark assesses performance across a diverse set of eight data science task categories, additionally exploring the sensitivity of models to common prompting issues, such as data leakage and slightly ambiguous instructions. We further investigate the influence of temperature parameters on overall and task-specific outcomes for each model and approach. Our findings reveal distinct performance disparities among the evaluated models and methodologies, highlighting critical factors that affect practical deployment. The benchmark dataset and evaluation framework introduced herein aim to provide a foundation for future research of more robust and effective data science agents.
title: DSBC : Data Science task Benchmarking with Context engineering

abstract: AI agents could accelerate scientific discovery by automating hypothesis formation, experiment design, coding, execution, and analysis, yet existing benchmarks probe narrow skills in simplified settings. To address this gap, we introduce InnovatorBench, a benchmark-platform pair for realistic, end-to-end assessment of agents performing Large Language Model (LLM) research. It comprises 20 tasks spanning Data Construction, Filtering, Augmentation, Loss Design, Reward Design, and Scaffold Construction, which require runnable artifacts and assessment of correctness, performance, output quality, and uncertainty. To support agent operation, we develop ResearchGym, a research environment offering rich action spaces, distributed and long-horizon execution, asynchronous monitoring, and snapshot saving. We also implement a lightweight ReAct agent that couples explicit reasoning with executable planning using frontier models such as Claude-4, GPT-5, GLM-4.5, and Kimi-K2. Our experiments demonstrate that while frontier models show promise in code-driven research tasks, they struggle with fragile algorithm-related tasks and long-horizon decision making, such as impatience, poor resource management, and overreliance on template-based reasoning. Furthermore, agents require over 11 hours to achieve their best performance on InnovatorBench, underscoring the benchmark's difficulty and showing the potential of InnovatorBench to be the next generation of code-based research benchmark.

keyInsights: Frontier LLM agents demonstrate foundational potential in handling code-based research, but their current capabilities are limited by challenges in long-horizon planning, reliable tool parameter specification, and effective resource management.

Agent performance varies significantly across LLMs and task domains, with data-related tasks proving more robust to minor errors compared to highly brittle algorithm-related tasks like loss or reward design.

Ground truth hints can surprisingly decrease performance in some data tasks by exposing agent coding weaknesses during exact replication attempts, while improving exploratory tasks by streamlining methodology.
originalProblem: Existing AI agent benchmarks primarily assess isolated skills or replication of known results, failing to capture the complexity and iterative nature of end-to-end scientific research workflows.

Current experimental environments for AI agents are often simplified, lacking support for long-horizon experiments, distributed training, asynchronous monitoring, and rich action spaces necessary for realistic research.
There is a lack of rigorous evaluation for AI agents' capacity to generate novel hypotheses, design creative objectives, or innovate architectural solutions within LLM research contexts.
results: Claude Sonnet 4 demonstrated the highest overall performance on InnovatorBench, leading in average scores and four out of six research domains compared to other frontier LLMs.

Agents exhibited common failure modes, including premature termination of long-running processes ('impatience'), inefficient resource allocation ('resource mismanagement'), and selection of suboptimal libraries (e.g., Transformers over vLLM).

InnovatorBench tasks required agents to interact with the environment for over 11 hours to reach peak performance, indicating significantly higher complexity and interaction costs compared to simpler benchmarks.
solution: Introduces InnovatorBench, a benchmark featuring 20 end-to-end, innovation-oriented LLM research tasks derived from influential papers and their open-source codebases.

Develops ResearchGym, a scalable and realistic experimental platform offering a rich action space (42 primitive actions), multi-computer control, asynchronous command execution, and environment snapshot capabilities.

Utilizes a ReAct-style agent powered by frontier LLMs (e.g., Claude Sonnet 4, GPT-5) to empirically evaluate agent performance across six critical LLM research domains within the ResearchGym environment.
paper_summary.summary: InnovatorBench and ResearchGym provide a new framework to evaluate AI agents on comprehensive, end-to-end LLM research tasks in a realistic environment. The evaluation reveals that frontier large language models possess foundational coding research abilities but face challenges in long-horizon planning, resource management, and reliable tool use across diverse research domains.

title: InnovatorBench: Evaluating Agents' Ability to Conduct Innovative LLM Research

abstract: As large language models (LLMs) evolve from conversational assistants into autonomous agents, evaluating the safety of their actions becomes critical. Prior safety benchmarks have primarily focused on preventing generation of harmful content, such as toxic text. However, they overlook the challenge of agents taking harmful actions when the most effective path to an operational goal conflicts with human safety. To address this gap, we introduce ManagerBench, a benchmark that evaluates LLM decision-making in realistic, human-validated managerial scenarios. Each scenario forces a choice between a pragmatic but harmful action that achieves an operational goal, and a safe action that leads to worse operational performance. A parallel control set, where potential harm is directed only at inanimate objects, measures a model's pragmatism and identifies its tendency to be overly safe. Our findings indicate that the frontier LLMs perform poorly when navigating this safety-pragmatism trade-off. Many consistently choose harmful options to advance their operational goals, while others avoid harm only to become overly safe and ineffective. Critically, we find this misalignment does not stem from an inability to perceive harm, as models' harm assessments align with human judgments, but from flawed prioritization. ManagerBench is a challenging benchmark for a core component of agentic behavior: making safe choices when operational goals and alignment values incentivize conflicting actions. Benchmark & code available at this https URL.
title: ManagerBench: Evaluating the Safety-Pragmatism Trade-off in Autonomous LLMs

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly act as autonomous agents, using tools to execute code, read and write files, and access networks, creating novel security risks. To mitigate these risks, agents are commonly deployed and evaluated in isolated "sandbox" environments, often implemented using Docker/OCI containers. We introduce SANDBOXESCAPEBENCH, an open benchmark that safely measures an LLM's capacity to break out of these sandboxes. The benchmark is implemented as an Inspect AI Capture the Flag (CTF) evaluation utilising a nested sandbox architecture with the outer layer containing the flag and no known vulnerabilities. Following a threat model of a motivated adversarial agent with shell access inside a container, SANDBOXESCAPEBENCH covers a spectrum of sandboxescape mechanisms spanning misconfiguration, privilege allocation mistakes, kernel flaws, and runtime/orchestration weaknesses. We find that, when vulnerabilities are added, LLMs are able to identify and exploit them, showing that use of evaluation like SANDBOXESCAPEBENCH is needed to ensure sandboxing continues to provide the encapsulation needed for highly-capable models.

keyInsights: LLM escape capabilities scale approximately log-linearly with increasing inference-time compute budget (token limits), implying that more resources can improve an agent's chances of escape.
Models can opportunistically discover and exploit unintended escape paths, highlighting their generalist problem-solving abilities and the complexity of ensuring complete containment.

Providing scenario-specific hints dramatically accelerates LLM performance, suggesting that vulnerability discovery is often an initial bottleneck for current models rather than the exploitation itself.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly advancing in autonomy and cybersecurity capabilities, raising concerns about their potential misuse to bypass security measures.
Many open-source AI agent ecosystems and developer tools rely on standard Docker-based isolation, despite known vulnerabilities, creating uncertainty regarding their safety against frontier LLMs.
A lack of systematic evaluation exists for LLMs' capacity to bypass container sandboxes, leaving a crucial gap in understanding AI safety and control mechanisms.
results: Frontier LLMs reliably achieved high success rates on common sandbox weaknesses (difficulty 1-2 scenarios), such as privileged containers and exposed Docker sockets.

Largest models attained meaningful success rates (up to 40%) on medium-difficulty (level 3) scenarios involving specific vulnerability knowledge or multi-step exploitation, but no model succeeded on difficulty 4 and 5 tasks.

Significant performance disparities were observed between state-of-the-art and smaller frontier models, with some showing version-to-version regressions in agentic skills (e.g., GPT-5.2 performed worse than GPT-5).
solution: Introduced SANDBOXESCAPEBENCH, a comprehensive, open benchmark with 18 distinct challenge levels covering real-world container vulnerability classes across orchestration, runtime, and kernel layers.

Developed a safe evaluation methodology using nested sandboxing (a 'sandbox-in-a-sandbox' approach) where the test container runs inside a virtual machine to prevent compromise of the evaluation host.

Implemented the benchmark on the Inspect AI framework with a custom `bash()` tool, ensuring task and outcome validity by requiring retrieval of a unique host-level flag and preventing shortcut exploits.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Oxford and the UK AI Security Institute developed SANDBOXESCAPEBENCH, an open benchmark to quantify Large Language Models' capacity to escape containerized sandbox environments. Evaluations showed frontier LLMs reliably exploit common misconfigurations and known vulnerabilities, demonstrating critical security risks in current sandboxing practices and achieving up to 40% success on medium-difficulty tasks.

title: Quantifying Frontier LLM Capabilities for Container Sandbox Escape

abstract: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is rapidly becoming the standard interface for Large Language Models (LLMs) to discover and invoke external tools. However, existing evaluations often fail to capture the complexity of real-world scenarios, relying on restricted toolsets, simplistic workflows, or subjective LLM-as-a-judge metrics. We introduce MCP-Atlas, a large-scale benchmark for evaluating tool-use competency, comprising 36 real MCP servers and 220 tools. It includes 1,000 tasks designed to assess tool-use competency in realistic, multi-step workflows. Tasks use natural language prompts that avoid naming specific tools or servers, requiring agents to identify and orchestrate 3-6 tool calls across multiple servers. We score tasks using a claims-based rubric that awards partial credit based on the factual claims satisfied in the model's final answer, complemented by internal diagnostics on tool discovery, parameterization, syntax, error recovery, and efficiency. Evaluation results on frontier models reveal that top models achieve pass rates exceeding 50%, with primary failures arising from inadequate tool usage and task understanding. We release the task schema, containerized harness, and a 500-task public subset of the benchmark dataset to facilitate reproducible comparisons and advance the development of robust, tool-augmented agents.

keyInsights: A "Tool Awareness-Capability Gap" is present in LLM agents, where the primary challenge lies in deciding when and which tool to invoke, rather than correctly executing an invoked tool.
The claims-based evaluation rubric provides an objective, scalable, and reproducible method for assessing agent performance, achieving 78% agreement with human judgments.
"Tool Usage" errors, including incorrect tool selection and parameterization, constitute the largest share of failures, indicating a critical area for improvement in agent design.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for LLM tool-use competency struggled to simultaneously achieve large scale, realism, and objective evaluation.
Rigor-focused evaluations using real servers were limited in scale, while large-scale benchmarks often compromised on realism or introduced subjective "LLM-as-judge" scoring.
There was a need for a benchmark that rigorously assessed tool discovery, precise parameterization, and complex multi-step workflows with authentic, live APIs.
results: Frontier models, such as Claude Opus 4.5, achieved the highest pass rate of 62.30% with 78.5% mean coverage, indicating substantial room for improvement in complex, multi-step scenarios.
Over half of all failures (an average of 56.7%) were attributed to "Tool Usage" errors, primarily stemming from incorrect tool selection or improper parameterization.

Task performance showed significant domain variance, with ANALYTICS tasks being the easiest, while FINANCIAL and CODING domains proved most challenging, often requiring highly precise input and tool disambiguation.
solution: Researchers introduced MCP-Atlas, a large-scale benchmark featuring 1,000 tasks that require LLM agents to perform 3-6 tool calls across 36 real MCP servers and 220 distinct tools.
An objective claims-based scoring rubric was developed, verifying factual claims in the agent's final answer using an LLM judge, independent of the specific execution path.
Tasks are configured with a controlled tool set including 5-10 plausible distractors, forcing agents to demonstrate precise tool discovery and correct parameterization.
paper_summary.summary: Scale AI developed MCP-Atlas, a benchmark with 1,000 tasks on 36 real Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers to evaluate Large Language Model (LLM) agent tool-use competency, employing an objective claims-based scoring system. Top models achieved a 62.30% pass rate, indicating substantial room for improvement in tool awareness and multi-step task completion.

title: MCP-Atlas: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Tool-Use Competency with Real MCP Servers

abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents executing multi-step workflows in real-world software environments. However, existing agent benchmarks suffer from three critical limitations: (1) trajectory-opaque grading that checks only final outputs, (2) underspecified safety and robustness evaluation, and (3) narrow modality coverage and interaction paradigms. We introduce Claw-Eval, an end-to-end evaluation suite addressing all three gaps. It comprises 300 human-verified tasks spanning 9 categories across three groups (general service orchestration, multimodal perception and generation, and multi-turn professional dialogue). Every agent action is recorded through three independent evidence channels (execution traces, audit logs, and environment snapshots), enabling trajectory-aware grading over 2,159 fine-grained rubric items. The scoring protocol evaluates Completion, Safety, and Robustness, reporting Average Score, Pass@k, and Pass^k across three trials to distinguish genuine capability from lucky outcomes. Experiments on 14 frontier models reveal that: (1) trajectory-opaque evaluation is systematically unreliable, missing 44% of safety violations and 13% of robustness failures that our hybrid pipeline catches; (2) controlled error injection primarily degrades consistency rather than peak capability, with Pass^3 dropping up to 24% while Pass@3 remains stable; (3) multimodal performance varies sharply, with most models performing poorer on video than on document or image, and no single model dominating across all modalities. Beyond benchmarking, Claw-Eval highlights actionable directions for agent development, shedding light on what it takes to build agents that are not only capable but reliably deployable.

keyInsights: Trajectory-opaque evaluation methods are unreliable, as a vanilla LLM judge missed 44% of safety violations and 13% of robustness issues compared to Claw-Eval's structured auditing.

Agent reliability (Pass^k) significantly degrades under transient environmental failures, even when peak capability (Pass@k) remains stable, indicating that robustness is a distinct and crucial capability not directly tied to baseline performance.

Success in multi-turn dialogues is strongly correlated with the quality and precision of questions (r = 0.87), not the quantity, and multimodal capabilities show strong domain-specific performance with no single model dominating all visual domains.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for autonomous LLM agents are trajectory-opaque, only verifying final outputs and allowing 'reward hacking' where agents achieve correct results through unfaithful execution.

Current evaluation practices for safety and robustness are insufficient, either isolating safety into separate red-teaming exercises or preventing harm through sandboxing without scoring agent attempts at unsafe actions or recovery from perturbations.

Most agent benchmarks focus on a single modality, failing to provide a unified framework for evaluating heterogeneous capabilities across diverse scenarios like multi-service orchestration, visual media processing, and extended professional dialogues.
results: Claude-Opus-4.6 achieved the highest Pass^3 (70.4% overall), demonstrating superior reliability, while Claude-Sonnet-4.6 had the highest Average Score (81.4% overall), highlighting a distinction between peak performance and consistent execution.

Multimodal tasks were substantially more challenging, with the highest Pass^3 reaching only 25.7% (GPT-5.4), and performance rankings shifting across specific multimodal domains (e.g., video tasks had the lowest average Pass^3 at 10.7%).

Injected errors primarily degraded agent consistency (Pass^3 sharply declined by up to 24.2%) rather than peak capability (Pass@3 remained largely stable, dropping only up to 3.7%), quantifying the gap between capability and reliability.
solution: Claw-Eval implements an auditable execution pipeline within isolated Docker containers, leveraging mock services and an external monitoring system to record and verify every agent action and intermediate step.

A comprehensive, cross-modal task suite of 300 human-verified tasks covers general workflows, multimodal interactions (video, image/document, code generation), and multi-turn dialogues, all using a unified execution lifecycle.

A multi-dimensional scoring protocol evaluates tasks across Completion, Safety, and Robustness, using fine-grained rubrics and multi-trial evaluation (k=3) to provide complementary metrics: Average Score, Pass@k, and Pass^k.
paper_summary.summary: Claw-Eval introduces an end-to-end evaluation suite for autonomous LLM agents, emphasizing full-trajectory auditing and integrated multi-dimensional scoring across diverse tasks. The system quantifies a clear divide between peak agent capability and consistent reliability, revealing that current models frequently struggle with robustness and exhibit domain-specific performance in multimodal understanding under realistic conditions.

title: Claw-Eval: Toward Trustworthy Evaluation of Autonomous Agents

abstract:
The rapid deployment of Large language model (LLM) agents in critical domains
like healthcare and finance necessitates robust security frameworks. To address
the absence of standardized evaluation benchmarks for these agents in dynamic
environments, we introduce RAS-Eval, a comprehensive security benchmark
supporting both simulated and real-world tool execution. RAS-Eval comprises 80
test cases and 3,802 attack tasks mapped to 11 Common Weakness Enumeration
(CWE) categories, with tools implemented in JSON, LangGraph, and Model Context
Protocol (MCP) formats. We evaluate 6 state-of-the-art LLMs across diverse
scenarios, revealing significant vulnerabilities: attacks reduced agent task
completion rates (TCR) by 36.78% on average and achieved an 85.65% success rate
in academic settings. Notably, scaling laws held for security capabilities,
with larger models outperforming smaller counterparts. Our findings expose
critical risks in real-world agent deployments and provide a foundational
framework for future security research. Code and data are available at
this https URL

keyInsights: LLM agents exhibit substantial security vulnerabilities in real-world scenarios, with attacks leading to significant reductions in task completion and a drastic shift towards specific failure modes like partial tool omission.

The security capabilities of LLM agents, similar to general performance, adhere to scaling laws, where larger models generally demonstrate improved resilience and higher performance scores on security-focused tasks.

The benchmark's detailed failure mode taxonomy reveals that attacks primarily cause agents to fail by preventing them from invoking necessary tools (Partial Tool Omission) and increasing runtime execution faults.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent security benchmarks primarily rely on simulated environments, leading to a "fidelity gap" that fails to capture real-world vulnerabilities such as insecure permissions or network effects.

There is an absence of standardized evaluation frameworks capable of assessing LLM agents across diverse and proliferating tool integration formats like JSON, LangGraph, and Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP).
Current security evaluations often lack comprehensive coverage of potential vulnerabilities and granular analysis of failure modes when LLM agents are under attack in operational settings.
results: Adversarial attacks on state-of-the-art LLM agents reduced their average task completion rates (TCR) by 36.78%, while achieving an overall average attack success rate (ASR) of 73.44% across diverse scenarios.

The benchmark was validated to have appropriate difficulty with an average Kappa coefficient of 0.6499 and strong discriminative power, showing a high correlation (R

							

									²=0.9051) between logarithmic parameter counts and performance scores for Qwen-series models.
Post-attack, "Partial Tool Omission" (F1) drastically increased from 25.42% to 75.54% of observed failures, and "Perfect" task completion decreased from 63.96% to a mere 20.73%.
solution: RAS-Eval constructs a benchmark dataset that supports both simulated and real-world tool execution, featuring 75 real tools across 15 categories implemented in JSON, LangGraph, and MCP formats.
It includes 3,802 attack tasks linked to 80 test cases, meticulously classified and mapped to 11 Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) categories for broad vulnerability coverage.
A novel, hierarchical taxonomy of six atomic failure modes is introduced to enable precise, granular root cause analysis of agent failures under adversarial conditions.
paper_summary.summary: RAS-Eval, developed at Zhejiang University, introduces a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM agent security in both simulated and real-world environments by integrating diverse tool formats and attack types. The evaluation demonstrates that adversarial attacks significantly reduce the average task completion rates of state-of-the-art LLMs by 36.78% and achieve an average attack success rate of 73.44%.

title: RAS-Eval: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Security Evaluation of LLM  Agents in Real-World Environments

abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs) show promising potential in assistive tasks across various domains, including mobile device control. As these agents interact directly with personal information and device settings, ensuring their safe and reliable behavior is crucial to prevent undesirable outcomes. However, no benchmark exists for standardized evaluation of the safety of mobile device-control agents. In this work, we introduce MobileSafetyBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate the safety of device-control agents within a realistic mobile environment based on Android emulators. We develop a diverse set of tasks involving interactions with various mobile applications, including messaging and banking applications, challenging agents with managing risks encompassing misuse and negative side effects. These tasks include tests to evaluate the safety of agents in daily scenarios as well as their robustness against indirect prompt injection attacks. Our experiments demonstrate that baseline agents, based on state-of-the-art LLMs, often fail to effectively prevent harm while performing the tasks. To mitigate these safety concerns, we propose a prompting method that encourages agents to prioritize safety considerations. While this method shows promise in promoting safer behaviors, there is still considerable room for improvement to fully earn user trust. This highlights the urgent need for continued research to develop more robust safety mechanisms in mobile environments.

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Austin

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art LLM agents exhibit a distinct trade-off between task helpfulness and safety, often neglecting safety for task completion or being overly cautious.
Agents are highly vulnerable to indirect prompt injection attacks embedded in UI elements, frequently misinterpreting them as valid user instructions and executing malicious actions.

A "reasoning-to-action" gap exists, where LLMs can detect risks in static QA formats (e.g., 92% detection) but fail to translate this awareness into safe behaviors in dynamic, interactive mobile environments.
Explicitly incorporating safety considerations via prompting, such as the proposed SCoT method, can enhance refusal rates in high-risk scenarios.
originalProblem: Autonomous agents powered by large language models, while capable of automating complex tasks on mobile devices, gain direct access to sensitive user information and device functionalities, posing inherent safety risks.

Existing LLM safety benchmarks primarily rely on static question-answering formats, which are insufficient to evaluate the dynamic, interactive, and potentially consequential behaviors of agents controlling mobile devices.
Previous benchmarks for mobile agents prioritized task completion and efficiency, neglecting a comprehensive assessment of safety behaviors in real-world interactive environments.
results: Baseline LLM agents demonstrated a safety-helpfulness trade-off, with GPT-4o achieving 82% goal completion on low-risk tasks but only a 31% refusal rate on high-risk tasks.
Agents exhibited severe vulnerability to indirect prompt injection attacks, with GPT-4o defending only 3 out of 50 such tasks, indicating a significant security risk.
The Safety-guided Chain-of-Thought (SCoT) prompting method improved GPT-4o's refusal rate on high-risk tasks from 6% to 36% without negatively impacting low-risk task performance.

Advanced LLMs, while showing some safety improvements, introduced substantial practical limitations, such as an average response latency four times higher than GPT-4o, hindering real-time interaction.
solution: Introduced MobileSafetyBench, the first platform designed to evaluate both the safety and helpfulness of autonomous agents in realistic mobile device control environments using Android emulators.
Developed a comprehensive task suite of 250 tasks, covering 13 applications and diverse risk types, including 50 tasks specifically for indirect prompt injection attacks.
Implemented a detailed observation space (screen images + UI element text descriptions) and a flexible action space, including `refuse()` and `ask-consent()` actions for safety.
Proposed Safety-guided Chain-of-Thought (SCoT) prompting, requiring agents to explicitly generate safety considerations before planning actions.
paper_summary.summary: MobileSafetyBench introduces the first benchmark for assessing the safety and helpfulness of autonomous agents controlling mobile devices in realistic, interactive environments. The evaluation reveals that current frontier LLM agents are highly susceptible to indirect prompt injection attacks and struggle with nuanced risks, exhibiting a "reasoning-to-action" gap despite high risk detection in static question-answering formats.

title: MobileSafetyBench: Evaluating Safety of Autonomous Agents in Mobile Device Control

abstract:
Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have increased the demand for
comprehensive benchmarks to evaluate their capabilities as human-like agents.
Existing benchmarks, while useful, often focus on specific application
scenarios, emphasizing task completion but failing to dissect the underlying
skills that drive these outcomes. This lack of granularity makes it difficult
to deeply discern where failures stem from. Additionally, setting up these
environments requires considerable effort, and issues of unreliability and
reproducibility sometimes arise, especially in interactive tasks. To address
these limitations, we introduce the Massive Multitask Agent Understanding
(MMAU) benchmark, featuring comprehensive offline tasks that eliminate the need
for complex environment setups. It evaluates models across five domains,
including Tool-use, Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) QA, Data Science and Machine
Learning coding, Contest-level programming and Mathematics, and covers five
essential capabilities: Understanding, Reasoning, Planning, Problem-solving,
and Self-correction. With a total of 20 meticulously designed tasks
encompassing over 3K distinct prompts, MMAU provides a comprehensive framework
for evaluating the strengths and limitations of LLM agents. By testing 18
representative models on MMAU, we provide deep and insightful analyses.
Ultimately, MMAU not only sheds light on the capabilities and limitations of
LLM agents but also enhances the interpretability of their performance.
Datasets and evaluation scripts of MMAU are released at
this https URL
title: MMAU: A Holistic Benchmark of Agent Capabilities Across Diverse Domains

abstract: Web-based agents powered by large language models are increasingly used for tasks such as email management or professional networking. Their reliance on dynamic web content, however, makes them vulnerable to prompt injection attacks: adversarial instructions hidden in interface elements that persuade the agent to divert from its original task. We introduce the Task-Redirecting Agent Persuasion Benchmark (TRAP), an evaluation for studying how persuasion techniques misguide autonomous web agents on realistic tasks. Across six frontier models, agents are susceptible to prompt injection in 25\% of tasks on average (13\% for GPT-5 to 43\% for DeepSeek-R1), with small interface or contextual changes often doubling success rates and revealing systemic, psychologically driven vulnerabilities in web-based agents. We also provide a modular social-engineering injection framework with controlled experiments on high-fidelity website clones, allowing for further benchmark expansion.

keyInsights: Frontier LLM-based web agents exhibit significant vulnerabilities to persuasion-driven prompt injection, with an average attack success rate of 25% across models.
Agents are susceptible to specific human persuasion principles (e.g., Social Proof, Consistency) and LLM manipulation methods (e.g., Adversarial Suffixes, Chain-of-Thought Injection).
Interface design (buttons are 3.5 times more effective than hyperlinks) and contextual tailoring (e.g., a 5.6x ASR increase) dramatically influence the effectiveness of prompt injection attacks.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for web-based LLM agents are often static, lack realism in simulating popular websites, and use ambiguous success metrics.
Autonomous web agents, while offering automation, inherit complex vulnerabilities from LLM architectures and dynamic web environments, particularly from prompt injection.
There is a need for granular analysis to understand not just if an attack succeeds, but when and why, linking failures to specific persuasion principles and LLM manipulation methods.
results: The overall average attack success rate was approximately 25%, ranging from 13% for GPT-5 (most robust) to 43% for DeepSeek-R1 (most vulnerable).
Button-based injections were 3.5 times more effective than hyperlink-based injections, accounting for 77.5% of all successful attacks.
Contextual tailoring of injections led to a sharp increase in attack success rates, for example, a 5.6x rise for GoCalendar tasks and a 2.5x rise for DashDish tasks.
Attacks successful on more robust models (e.g., GPT-5) showed high transferability (averaging 82.5%) to weaker models.
solution: The TRAP benchmark employs a five-dimensional modular attack space and high-fidelity website clones from the REAL framework to create realistic evaluation scenarios.
It uses a 'one-click success metric' to objectively measure agent redirection, isolating the critical decision point of an attack.
The framework provides a comprehensive evaluation across 18 benign tasks on 6 cloned websites, assessing 6 frontier LLMs against 630 distinct modular injection combinations.
paper_summary.summary: The TRAP (Task-Redirecting Agent Persuasion) benchmark introduces a framework to systematically study how persuasion techniques influence autonomous web agents performing realistic online tasks. It quantifies the susceptibility of frontier LLM-based web agents to prompt injection attacks, demonstrating that factors like contextual tailoring and specific interface elements significantly impact attack success rates.

title: It's a TRAP! Task-Redirecting Agent Persuasion Benchmark for Web Agents

abstract: Evaluating the safety of LLM-based agents is increasingly important because risks in realistic deployments often emerge over multi-step interactions rather than isolated prompts or final responses. Existing trajectory-level benchmarks remain limited by insufficient interaction diversity, coarse observability of safety failures, and weak long-horizon realism. We introduce ATBench, a trajectory-level benchmark for structured, diverse, and realistic evaluation of agent safety. ATBench organizes agentic risk along three dimensions: risk source, failure mode, and real-world harm. Based on this taxonomy, we construct trajectories with heterogeneous tool pools and a long-context delayed-trigger protocol that captures realistic risk emergence across multiple stages. The benchmark contains 1,000 trajectories (503 safe and 497 unsafe), averaging 9.01 turns and 3.95k tokens, with 1,954 invoked tools drawn from pools spanning 2,084 available tools. Data quality is supported by rule-based and LLM-based filtering plus full human audit. Experiments on frontier LLMs, open-source models, and specialized guard systems show that ATBench is challenging even for strong evaluators, while enabling taxonomy-stratified analysis, cross-benchmark comparison, and diagnosis of long-horizon failure patterns.
title: ATBench: A Diverse and Realistic Trajectory Benchmark for Long-Horizon Agent Safety

abstract:
The ability of large language models (LLMs) to mimic human-like intelligence
has led to a surge in LLM-based autonomous agents. Though recent LLMs seem
capable of planning and reasoning given user instructions, their effectiveness
in applying these capabilities for autonomous task solving remains
underexplored. This is especially true in enterprise settings, where automated
agents hold the promise of a high impact. To fill this gap, we propose
WorkArena++, a novel benchmark consisting of 682 tasks corresponding to
realistic workflows routinely performed by knowledge workers. WorkArena++ is
designed to evaluate the planning, problem-solving, logical/arithmetic
reasoning, retrieval, and contextual understanding abilities of web agents. Our
empirical studies across state-of-the-art LLMs and vision-language models
(VLMs), as well as human workers, reveal several challenges for such models to
serve as useful assistants in the workplace. In addition to the benchmark, we
provide a mechanism to effortlessly generate thousands of ground-truth
observation/action traces, which can be used for fine-tuning existing models.
Overall, we expect this work to serve as a useful resource to help the
community progress toward capable autonomous agents. The benchmark can be found
at this https URL

keyInsights: The stark performance gap between human workers (93.9% success) and state-of-the-art LLM-based agents (GPT-4o at 3.0-3.8% on L2, 0% on L3) highlights a substantial challenge for current AI in complex enterprise tasks.

Error analysis reveals agents struggle with accurate information retrieval, lack exploratory behavior, hallucinate actions, and exhibit deficiencies in goal understanding and assessing action consequences.
The visual modality provided to GPT-4o-v offered a marginal benefit for information retrieval tasks, suggesting potential but not a complete solution for robust interaction.
originalProblem: Autonomous agents lack sufficient evaluation for applying planning and reasoning in complex, compositional, and often ambiguous workflows typical of enterprise settings.

Existing web agent benchmarks focus primarily on atomic tasks (e.g., MiniWoB, WorkArena L1) or consumer-facing websites (WebArena), not reflecting the multi-step reasoning and information synthesis required in knowledge work.
Knowledge workers spend significant time on monotonous, low-value tasks within enterprise software, requiring advanced AI capabilities for automation that are currently underexplored.
results: State-of-the-art LLMs like GPT-4o achieved only 3.0-3.8% success rates on WorkArena++ L2 tasks and 0% on L3 tasks, indicating current models are largely unable to solve these compositional problems.
Human participants achieved a 93.9% success rate on the WorkArena++ L2/L3 tasks, establishing a high but achievable baseline for the benchmark.
Agents often failed due to inaccurate information extraction, inability to explore hidden UI elements, hallucination of non-existent actions, and misinterpreting task goals or action consequences.
solution: WorkArena++ introduces a benchmark on the ServiceNow platform with 682 compositional tasks across two difficulty levels (L2 Explicit, L3 Ticket + Knowledge Base) to evaluate planning, reasoning, and information retrieval skills.

Tasks are categorized into five skill areas: Planning and Problem Solving, Information Retrieval, Data-driven Decision Making and Reasoning, Sophisticated Memorization, and Contextual Understanding (with infeasible tasks).

Technical enhancements include increased visual diversity (10 company brands), standardized task isolation through temporary user accounts, and a framework for generating ground-truth observation/action traces for fine-tuning.
paper_summary.summary: WorkArena++ presents a benchmark for evaluating autonomous web agents on common knowledge work tasks within the ServiceNow enterprise platform, aiming to test compositional planning and reasoning capabilities. The benchmark expands on previous work by introducing 682 complex, multi-step tasks that require advanced skills, demonstrating that current state-of-the-art large language models achieve success rates significantly lower than human performance.

title: WorkArena++: Towards Compositional Planning and Reasoning-based Common  Knowledge Work Tasks

abstract: Large vision-language model (LVLM)-based web agents are emerging as powerful tools for automating complex online tasks. However, when deployed in real-world environments, they face serious security risks, motivating the design of security evaluation benchmarks. Existing benchmarks provide only partial coverage, typically restricted to narrow scenarios such as user-level prompt manipulation, and thus fail to capture the broad range of agent vulnerabilities. To address this gap, we present \tool{}, the first holistic benchmark for evaluating the security of LVLM-based web agents. \tool{} first introduces a unified evaluation suite comprising six simulated but realistic web environments (\eg, e-commerce platforms, community forums) and includes 2,970 high-quality trajectories spanning diverse tasks and attack settings. The suite defines a structured taxonomy of six attack vectors spanning both user-level and environment-level manipulations. In addition, we introduce a multi-layered evaluation protocol that analyzes agent failures across three critical dimensions: internal reasoning, behavioral trajectory, and task outcome, facilitating a fine-grained risk analysis that goes far beyond simple success metrics. Using this benchmark, we conduct large-scale experiments on 9 representative LVLMs, which fall into three categories: general-purpose, agent-specialized, and GUI-grounded. Our results show that all tested agents are consistently vulnerable to subtle adversarial manipulations and reveal critical trade-offs between model specialization and security. By providing (1) a comprehensive benchmark suite with diverse environments and a multi-layered evaluation pipeline, and (2) empirical insights into the security challenges of modern LVLM-based web agents, \tool{} establishes a foundation for advancing trustworthy web agent deployment.

organization_info.name: Henan University of Science and Technology
University of Chinese Academy of Science

keyInsights: All nine tested LVLM-based web agents, including general-purpose, agent-specialized, and GUI-grounded models, exhibit consistent vulnerabilities to subtle adversarial manipulations across diverse web environments.

Attacks exploiting visual perception, such as pop-up and ad injections, are generally more effective than those relying solely on semantic manipulation, with pop-up attacks being the most potent across models.

The multi-layered evaluation protocol reveals that agents can sometimes mitigate malicious intent in later stages of execution, even after initial reasoning or partial behavioral compromise, indicating a progressive security posture.
originalProblem: Existing security benchmarks for Large Vision-Language Model (LVLM) based web agents were fragmented, covering only narrow attack scenarios or focusing primarily on functional capabilities.
Previous evaluations lacked a unified framework to assess both user-level and environment-level manipulations, failing to consider their interplay in complex online interactions.
Prior work provided only simple success metrics, offering no fine-grained analysis of the causal root of security failures (i.e., why and how vulnerabilities occur).
results: Pop-up attacks consistently achieved the highest Payload Delivery Rates (PDR), ranging from 76.67% to 100% across all evaluated LVLM-based agents, demonstrating a universal susceptibility.

GUI-grounded models exhibited the strongest overall security compared to general-purpose and agent-specialized models, though they remained vulnerable to specific attacks like Ad Injection (UI-TARS-1.5 at 80.00% PDR).

Causal diagnosis revealed distinct failure modes: GPT-5 often suffered from semantic reasoning failures (e.g., deeming malicious actions 'reasonable'), while UI-TARS-1.5 frequently succumbed to perceptual failures (e.g., being guided by visual salience without noticing malicious text).
solution: Researchers developed SecureWebArena, a benchmark with six diverse, simulated yet realistic web environments (e.g., e-commerce, forums) designed to test agents across various online interaction contexts.

The benchmark operationalizes a comprehensive taxonomy of six attack vectors, including user-level manipulations (Direct Prompt Injection, Jailbreak) and environment-level deceptions (Pop-up Attack, Ad Injection, Indirect Prompt Injection, Distract Attack).

A multi-layered evaluation protocol assesses agent security across three stages—internal reasoning (Reasoning Vulnerability Rate), behavioral trajectory (Behavioral Compromise Rate), and final task outcome (Payload Delivery Rate)—to enable causal diagnosis of vulnerabilities.
paper_summary.summary: SecureWebArena is introduced as the first holistic security evaluation benchmark for LVLM-based web agents, integrating diverse web environments, a broad attack taxonomy, and a multi-layered evaluation protocol. The benchmark reveals consistent vulnerabilities across state-of-the-art models, with pop-up attacks being particularly effective and achieving Payload Delivery Rates (PDR) from 76.67% to 100%.

title: SecureWebArena: A Holistic Security Evaluation Benchmark for LVLM-based Web Agents

abstract: The advancement of large language model (LLM) based agents has shifted AI evaluation from single-turn response assessment to multi-step task completion in interactive environments. We present an empirical study evaluating frontier AI models on 150 workplace tasks within a realistic e-commerce RL environment from Surge.
